5-1.q: A company has established policies that emphasize hiring competent people and providing them with professional training. This aligns most closely with which one of the five major components of COSO’s internal control framework?
o: Information and communication.
Control environment.
Risk assessment.
Monitoring.
5-1.e: Establishing policies that emphasize hiring competent people and providing them with professional training aligns most closely with the control environment component of COSO's internal control framework. Organizations with effective control environments have several characteristics, one of which is that they demonstrate a commitment to attract, develop, and retain competent individuals. The company needs to have competent personnel in order for tasks to be accomplished in accordance with the company’s objectives and plans for achievement of those objectives. In order to have competent personnel, management should specify the knowledge and skills required for each position. Formal or informal job descriptions should specify the competence level needed for each job, and the company should make every effort to hire and retain competent people and to train them when necessary.
wrongWhy: Establishing policies that emphasize hiring competent people and providing them with professional training does not align with the information and communication component of COSO's internal control framework. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Establishing policies that emphasize hiring competent people and providing them with professional training does not align with the risk assessment component of COSO's internal control framework. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Establishing policies that emphasize hiring competent people and providing them with professional training does not align with the monitoring activities component of COSO's internal control framework. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-1.q: A control activity is
o: an action that is taken by an auditor to obtain evidence.
a method of recording, summarizing and reporting financial information.
the support that the board of directors provides to the audit committee.
an activity that helps to ensure that management’s instructions intended to limit risks to the achievement of the organization’s objectives are carried out.
5-1.e: A control activity is an action established by policies and procedures that helps to ensure that management’s instructions intended to limit risks to the achievement of the organization’s objectives are carried out.
wrongWhy: An action that is taken by an auditor to obtain evidence is not a control activity. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
A control activity is not a method of recording, summarizing and reporting financial information. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
The support that the board of directors provides to the audit committee is not a control activity. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-1.q: Accounting controls are concerned with the safeguarding of assets and the reliability of financial records. Consequently, these controls are designed to provide reasonable assurance that all of the following take place except
o: Compliance with methods and procedures ensuring operational efficiency and adherence to managerial policies.
Permitting access to assets in accordance with management's authorization.
Comparing recorded assets with existing assets at periodic intervals and taking appropriate action with respect to differences.
Executing transactions in accordance with management's general or specific authorization.
5-1.e: Compliance with methods and procedures ensuring operational efficiency and adherence to managerial policies is an objective of operational control, not accounting control.
wrongWhy: The safeguarding of assets and reliability of financial records requires that management control who can have access to the assets to be safeguarded.

The safeguarding of assets and reliability of financial records requires that physical assets be compared with recorded assets at periodic intervals, and that action be taken to resolve any differences.
The safeguarding of assets and reliability of financial records requires that transactions be executed in accordance with management's general or specific authorization.

5-1.q: To minimize the risk that agents in the purchasing department will use their positions for personal gain, the organization should
o: Direct the purchasing department to maintain records on purchase prices paid, with review of such being required each 6 months.
Rotate purchasing agent assignments periodically.
Specify that all items purchased must pass value-per-unit-of-cost reviews.
Request internal auditors to confirm selected purchases and accounts payable.
5-1.e: Rotating purchasing agent assignments periodically will limit the risk of agents using their positions for personal gain, because it will discourage long-term agent relationships with particular vendors.
wrongWhy: Reviewing records on purchase prices paid would not prevent purchasing agents from using their positions for personal gain.
Value-per-unit-of-cost reviews would not prevent purchasing agents from using their positions for personal gain.
Requesting confirmation by auditors of selected purchases and accounts payable would not prevent purchasing agents from using their positions for personal gain.

5-1.q: An internal audit of the payroll function revealed several instances in which a payroll clerk had added fictitious employees to the payroll and deposited the checks in accounts of close relatives. What control should have prevented such actions?
o: Using time cards and attendance records in the computation of employee gross earnings.
Having the treasurer's office sign payroll checks.
Establishing a policy to deal with close relatives working in the same department.
Allowing changes to the payroll to be authorized only by the personnel department.
5-1.e: Only the personnel department should be authorized to make changes to the payroll, while only the payroll department should process payroll checks. Furthermore, to prevent an unauthorized person from adding a name of a fictitious employee to the payroll, payroll records should be reconciled with the active employee list from the personnel department each payday.
wrongWhy: Using time cards and attendance records would not prevent a payroll clerk from adding fictitious employees to the payroll and keeping and depositing their paychecks.
Having the treasurer's office sign payroll checks would not prevent a payroll clerk from adding fictitious employees to the payroll and keeping and depositing their paychecks.
Establishing a policy for the hiring of close relatives would not prevent a payroll clerk from adding fictitious employees to the payroll and keeping and depositing their paychecks.

5-1.q: The reporting of accounting information plays a central role in the regulation of business operations. The importance of sound internal control practices is underscored by the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act of 1977 which requires publicly owned U.S. corporations to maintain systems of internal control that meet certain minimum standards. Preventive controls are an integral part of virtually all accounting processing systems, and much of the information generated by the accounting system is used for preventive control purposes. Which one of the following is not an essential element of a sound preventive control system?
o: Separation of responsibilities for the recording, custodial, and authorization functions.
Implementation of state-of-the-art software and hardware.
Documentation of policies and procedures.
Sound personnel practices.
5-1.e: Implementation of state-of-the-art software and hardware is not necessary for an organization to have a sound control system.
wrongWhy: Separation of responsibilities for the recording, custodial, and authorization functions is an essential element of a sound preventive control system, because without such separation of responsibilities, a person could commit a fraud and conceal it.

Documented policies and procedures are an important part of a sound control system. Formal and clearly communicated policies and procedures that result in shared values and teamwork should be followed at all times, without exception.

Sound personnel practices contribute to sound control systems. Organizations with effective control environments transmit guidance to their employees both verbally and by example, communicating the entity's values, standards and code of conduct; and they follow up on violations. There are mechanisms to encourage employee reporting of suspected violations, and disciplinary actions are taken when employees fail to report them. Formal and clearly communicated policies and procedures that result in shared values and teamwork are followed at all times, without exception. The competence level needed for particular jobs is specified, competent people are hired and retained, and authority and responsibility are appropriately assigned. Internal control is an explicit or implicit part of everyone's job description, and all individuals in the organization realize that they will be held accountable.

5-1.q: Components) The primary responsibility for establishing and maintaining internal control rests with
o: The treasurer.
The internal auditor.
The controller.
Management.
5-1.e: Internal control is a method, or process, that is carried out by an entity's board of directors, CEO, senior management and other personnel, and designed to provide reasonable assurance that the company's objectives will be achieved.
wrongWhy: The treasurer does not have the primary responsibility for establishing and maintaining internal control.
Though the internal auditor does a lot of work in respect to the internal control system, the internal auditor is not responsible for establishing and maintaining internal control.
The controller does not have the primary responsibility for establishing and maintaining internal control.

5-1.q: Components) Internal control can provide only reasonable assurance of achieving entity control objectives because
o: management monitors internal control.
the cost of internal control should not exceed its benefits.
the auditor’s primary responsibility is the detection of fraud.
the board of directors is active and independent.
5-1.e: Internal control procedures can provide reasonable assurance only—not absolute assurance and not a guarantee—to the entity’s board of directors and senior management that the company’s objectives will be achieved. This statement reflects the fundamental concepts that (1) the cost of an internal control system should not exceed the expected benefits, and (2) the overall impact of a control procedure should not hinder operating efficiency.
wrongWhy: Management certainly should monitor internal control. However, that is not the reason that internal control can provide only reasonable assurance of achieving entity control objectives. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The auditor's primary responsibility is not the detection of fraud. The purpose of an external audit is for the auditor to provide an opinion as to whether the financial statements of the organization have been prepared correctly, according to the accounting standards under which they have been prepared. Furthermore, nothing that an external auditor does causes internal control to be able to provide only reasonable assurance of achieving entity control objectives. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The board of directors certainly should be active and independent. However, that is not the reason that internal control can provide only reasonable assurance of achieving entity control objectives. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-1.q: All of the following would be part of a factory’s control system to prevent release of wastewater that does not meet discharge standards except:
o: Performing chemical analysis of the water before discharge for components specified in the permit.
Establishing a preventive maintenance program for the factory’s pretreatment system.
Periodically flushing sinks and floor drains with a large volume of clean water to ensure pollutants prohibited to be disposed of down the drain are sufficiently diluted.
Specifying (by policy, training, and advisory signs) which substances may be disposed of via sinks and floor drains within the factory.
5-1.e: This question is concerned with complying with pollutant discharge regulations. It relates to the definition of internal control as providing reasonable assurance that the company’s objectives relating to compliance will be achieved. If discharge standards do not permit the disposal of a pollutant down the drain, dilution of it after it has been poured down the drain does not correct the violation.
wrongWhy: Each of these individual controls, and probably others as well, help management achieve its objective of preventing the release of wastewater that does not meet permit limits or other conditions. These three controls each approach the risk in different ways. Analytical results are the criteria for the decision to discharge; keeping pollutants out of the wastewater will help reduce concentrations and the degree of pretreatment needed; and breakdown of pretreatment equipment is less likely to occur if a preventive maintenance program is in place.

Each of these individual controls, and probably others as well, help management achieve its objective of preventing the release of wastewater that does not meet permit limits or other conditions. These three controls each approach the risk in different ways. Analytical results are the criteria for the decision to discharge; keeping pollutants out of the wastewater will help reduce concentrations and the degree of pretreatment needed; and breakdown of pretreatment equipment is less likely to occur if a preventive maintenance program is in place.

Each of these individual controls, and probably others as well, help management achieve its objective of preventing the release of wastewater that does not meet permit limits or other conditions. These three controls each approach the risk in different ways. Analytical results are the criteria for the decision to discharge; keeping pollutants out of the wastewater will help reduce concentrations and the degree of pretreatment needed; and breakdown of pretreatment equipment is less likely to occur if a preventive maintenance program is in place.

5-1.q: Components) Which one of the following statements in regard to internal controls is not true?
o: Control activities are the policies and procedures that help ensure that management directives are carried out.
Monitoring is a process that penalizes managers for breakdowns in internal control.
Control environment is the foundation for all other components of internal control, providing discipline and structure.
Risk assessment is the entity's identification and analysis of relevant risks to the achievement of its objectives.
5-1.e: This statement is not true. Monitoring is the process of evaluating the controls over time to determine whether the components of the internal control system are present and functioning and revisiting previously identified problems to make sure they have been corrected.
wrongWhy: This statement is true. Control activities are the actions established by policies and procedures that help ensure that management directives intended to limit risks to the achievement of the organization's objectives are carried out.
This statement is true. The control environment includes the standards, processes, and structures that provide the foundation for all of the other components of internal control.
This statement is true. Risk assessment is the process of identifying, analyzing, and managing the risks that have the potential to prevent the entity from achieving its objectives.

5-1.q: Components) Which one of the following auditor findings would most likely raise a red flag about a company’s internal control environment?
o: The board nominations committee selects only independent directors.
Only select committees of the board have access to outside attorneys.
The company has an established independent audit committee.
The role of the company’s CEO and board chairman are separate.
5-1.e: All of the committees of the board should have access to outside attorneys. If they do not, that would be a red flag for the auditors.
wrongWhy: This is a good thing for the company to have done and would not raise a red flag.
This is a good thing for the company to have done and would not raise a red flag.
This is a good thing for the company to have done and would not raise a red flag.

5-1.q: When management of the sales department has the opportunity to override the system of internal controls of the accounting department, a weakness exists in
o: information and communication.
monitoring.
risk management.
the control environment.
5-1.e: The control environment is a component of internal control. The control environment involves the structures, authorities, and responsibilities of employees. The control environment should include commitments to integrity, ethical values, and competence, and it should include a system to hold people accountable for their internal control responsibilities. If a manager can override (disregard) the internal control system, that is a weakness of the control environment because a manager overriding the internal control system violates the manager’s internal control responsibilities.
wrongWhy: Information and communication is a component of internal control. It is the process of identifying, capturing, and sharing relevant information to enable people to carry out their internal control responsibilities. A manager having the ability to override (disregard) the internal control system is not a weakness in the information and communication of internal controls.

Monitoring activities are a component of internal control. Monitoring is the overseeing by management of the internal control system's performance over time to determine whether the components of internal control are present and functioning. A manager having the ability to override (disregard) the internal control system is not a weakness in the monitoring of internal controls.

Risk management is forecasting and evaluating risks and identifying procedures to mitigate or avoid them. A manager having the ability to override (disregard) the internal control system is a risk, but it is not a weakness in risk management.

5-1.q: Which one of the following statements is correct?
o: Residual risk remains after management has implemented internal controls.
Inherent risk can only be addressed by top management.
Fraud can be eliminated by internal controls.
Residual risks can only be assessed but not protected against.
5-1.e: The definition of residual risk is "the level of risk that remains after management has taken action to mitigate a risk" according to Enterprise Risk Management: Frameworks, Elements and Integration, published by the Institute of Management Accountants (IMA) as part of their Statements on Management Accounting series. Internal control cannot provide a guarantee that an organization will achieve its financial reporting, operational, and compliance objectives. No matter how well designed and operated it is, internal control can provide only reasonable assurance to management and the board of directors that the organization’s objectives will be achieved. Controls may fail because of human error, they may be circumvented by collusion, or management may override internal control procedures. Therefore, the level of risk that remains after management has implemented internal controls is the residual risk.
wrongWhy: Inherent risk is the susceptibility to a material mistake that exists “just because.” It is risk that is natural, assuming no controls are in place. Action to address inherent risk is not limited to top management.

Fraud cannot be eliminated by internal controls. No internal control can provide a guarantee that fraud will not occur. For example, despite segregation of duties, two or more employees could collude with one another (work together to conspire) to commit fraud, covering for one another and, presumably, sharing the proceeds. Or a manager could override an internal control and take advantage of the opportunity to commit fraud.

Residual risk is "the level of risk that remains after management has taken action to mitigate a risk," according to Enterprise Risk Management: Frameworks, Elements and Integration, published by the Institute of Management Accountants (IMA) as part of their Statements on Management Accounting series. An example of a residual risk is the annual deductible amount on an insurance policy that the insured must pay before the insurance will reimburse a loss. Although the insured must pay it each year if a loss occurs, the insured can set up an allowance for the deductible amount it expects to owe each year and/or set aside funds to cover it. Therefore, although residual risk is risk that remains, it can be protected against.

5-1.q: Which one of the following is an example of a preventive control?
o: Audits of payroll accounts and disbursements.
Supervisory review of monthly bank reconciliations.
Reconciliation of petty cash accounts.
Supervisory review and approval of payroll information before disbursement.
5-1.e: Preventive controls are controls designed to avoid an unintended event such as an error or fraud before it occurs. Supervisory review and approval of payroll information before disbursement can prevent errors in the payroll before the payments are made, so it is a preventive control.
wrongWhy: Preventive controls are controls designed to avoid an unintended event such as an error or fraud before it occurs. Audits of payroll accounts and disbursements are a detective control because they are designed to discover an unintended event after it has occurred but before the ultimate objective has occurred, for example before financial statements are issued.

Preventive controls are controls designed to avoid an unintended event such as an error or fraud before it occurs. Supervisory review of monthly bank reconciliations is a detective control because it is designed to discover an unintended event after it has occurred but before the ultimate objective has occurred, for example before financial statements are issued.

Preventive controls are controls designed to avoid an unintended event such as an error or fraud before it occurs. Reconciliation of petty cash accounts is a detective control because it is designed to discover an unintended event after it has occurred but before the ultimate objective has occurred, for example before financial statements are issued.

5-1.q: Components) When an organization has strong internal control, management can expect various benefits. The benefit least likely to occur is
o: Reduced cost of an external audit.
Availability of reliable data for decision-making purposes.
Some assurance of compliance with the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002.
Elimination of employee fraud.
5-1.e: While strong internal control can limit employee fraud, complete elimination of employee fraud is not possible, and so this cannot be considered a benefit of a strong internal control system.
wrongWhy: Reduced cost of external audits is a benefit that is likely to occur as a result of strong internal control.
Strong internal control will result in the benefit of better, more accurate data for decision making.
Strong internal control will result in some assurance of compliance with the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002, and thus that is a benefit.

5-1.q: A restaurant food chain has over 680 restaurants. All food orders for each restaurant are required to be input into an electronic device which records all food orders by food servers and transmits the order to the kitchen for preparation. All food servers are responsible for collecting cash for all their orders and must turn in cash at the end of their shift equal to the sales value of food ordered for their I.D. number. The manager then reconciles the cash received for the day with the computerized record of food orders generated. All differences are investigated immediately by the restaurant. Corporate headquarters has established monitoring controls to determine when an individual restaurant might not be recording all its revenue and transmitting the applicable cash to the corporate headquarters. Which one of the following would be the best example of a monitoring control?
o: All food orders must be entered on the computer, and segregation of duties is maintained between the food servers and the cooks.
The restaurant manager reconciles the cash received with the food orders recorded on the computer.
Cash is transmitted to corporate headquarters on a daily basis.
Management prepares a detailed analysis of gross margin per store and investigates any store that shows a significantly lower gross margin.
5-1.e: There are five interrelated components that comprise internal control. They are: (1) control environment, (2) risk assessment, (3) control activities, (4) information and communication, and (5) monitoring. Monitoring is an activity of management. Monitoring assesses the quality of the internal control system's performance over time. Monitoring can be done in two ways: (1) through ongoing monitoring during normal operations, and (2) separate evaluations by management with the assistance of the internal audit activity. If monitoring is done regularly during normal operations, it lessens the need for separate evaluations. When management prepares a detailed analysis of gross margin per store and investigates any store that shows a significantly lower gross margin, it is performing a monitoring activity.
wrongWhy: There are five interrelated components that comprise internal control. They are: (1) control environment, (2) risk assessment, (3) control activities, (4) information and communication, and (5) monitoring. Monitoring is an activity of management. Monitoring assesses the quality of the internal control system's performance over time. Monitoring can be done in two ways: (1) through ongoing monitoring during normal operations, and (2) separate evaluations by management with the assistance of the internal audit activity. If monitoring is done regularly during normal operations, it lessens the need for separate evaluations. Segregation of duties is a control activity which serves as a preventive control, because it is intended to prevent the occurrence of an unwanted event. Therefore, it does not represent a monitoring activity of management.

There are five interrelated components that comprise internal control. They are: (1) control environment, (2) risk assessment, (3) control activities, (4) information and communication, and (5) monitoring. Monitoring is an activity of management. Monitoring assesses the quality of the internal control system's performance over time. Monitoring can be done in two ways: (1) through ongoing monitoring during normal operations, and (2) separate evaluations by management with the assistance of the internal audit activity. If monitoring is done regularly during normal operations, it lessens the need for separate evaluations. The manager's reconciliation of cash received with food orders entered is a control activity. A reconciliation is a detective control activity, because it is intended to detect the occurrence of an unwanted event. However, it does not represent a monitoring activity of management.

There are five interrelated components that comprise internal control. They are: (1) control environment, (2) risk assessment, (3) control activities, (4) information and communication, and (5) monitoring. Monitoring is an activity of management. Monitoring assesses the quality of the internal control system's performance over time. Monitoring can be done in two ways: (1) through ongoing monitoring during normal operations, and (2) separate evaluations by management with the assistance of the internal audit activity. If monitoring is done regularly during normal operations, it lessens the need for separate evaluations. Daily transmission of cash to corporate headquarters is a control activity which serves as an operational control.

5-1.q: The basic concepts implicit in internal accounting controls include the following. The cost of the system should not exceed benefits expected to be attained. The overall impact of the control procedure should not hinder operating efficiency. Which one of the following internal accounting controls recognizes these two factors?
o: Management responsibility.
Reasonable assurance.
Methods of data processing.
Limitations.
5-1.e: The goal of internal control is to provide reasonable assurance that the company's objectives will be achieved in the areas of (1) effectiveness and efficiency of operations, (2) reliability of financial reporting, and (3) compliance with applicable laws and regulations. It is not the goal of internal control to provide a guarantee that the company's objectives will be achieved in these areas. The idea of reasonable assurance recognizes that it is not possible for an internal control system to guarantee that it will detect every error or prevent every error. This limitation exists because of cost/benefit issues and because controls should not be so cumbersome that they prevent the business from operating. As a result, not every possible control will be implemented. Some may not be implemented because management has decided that the cost to do them would be greater than any potential loss that could result from not doing them. Some may not be implemented because management has decided that to do them would hinder operating efficiency too much. Because internal control is not expected to provide a guarantee but only to provide reasonable assurance, it is reasonable to omit some control procedures for those reasons.
wrongWhy: This answer is not correct. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
This answer is not correct. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
This answer is not correct. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

5-1.q: Effective internal control
o: Cannot be circumvented by management.
Eliminates risk and potential loss to the organization.
Reduces the need for management to review exception reports on a day-to-day basis.
Is unaffected by changing circumstances and conditions encountered by the organization.
5-1.e: An effective internal control structure should prevent exceptions as well as detect exceptions after the fact. Thus, with an effective internal control structure, management's need to review exception reports daily should be reduced — not because monitoring becomes unnecessary, but because strong preventive controls mean fewer exceptions appear in those reports. Note: the COSO Monitoring component still requires periodic exception report review as a key detective control. The other options are incorrect: management can override controls (option A); internal control provides reasonable assurance, not elimination of risk (option B); and effective controls must adapt to changing circumstances (option D).
wrongWhy: Internal controls can be overridden by managers.
No internal control structure, no matter how effective, can guarantee the complete elimination of risk and potential loss to the organization.
As circumstances and conditions change, changes in internal controls are required.

5-1.q: Which of the following best describes the concept of control?
o: Management regularly discharges personnel who do not perform up to expectations.
Control represents specific procedures that accountants and auditors design to ensure the correctness of processing.
Control procedures should be designed from the "bottom up" to ensure attention to detail.
Management takes action to enhance the likelihood that established goals and objectives will be achieved.
5-1.e: A control is any action taken by management to enhance the likelihood that established goals and objectives will be achieved.
wrongWhy: This is not the definition of a control.
Control encompasses much more than controls designed to ensure the correctness of processing. Furthermore, control is designed and instituted by management, not by accountants or auditors.
While control procedures may be designed from the bottom up, the concept of control flows from the top down.

5-1.q: Components) Which of the following is not a component of internal control?
o: Control activities.
Risk assessment.
Integration.
Control environment.
5-1.e: Integration is not a component of internal control. The components of internal control are: Control environment Risk assessment Control activities Information and communication Monitoring activities
wrongWhy: Control activities are a component of internal control.
Risk assessment is a component of internal control.
The control environment is a component of internal control.

5-1.q: The procedure requiring preparation of a prelisting of incoming cash receipts, with copies of the prelist going to the cashier and to accounting, is an example of which type of control?
o: Preventive.
Detective.
Directive.
Corrective.
5-1.e: Prelisting incoming cash receipts is a preventive control designed to prevent undesirable events from occurring. In this case, an undesirable event is the disappearance of cash payments. The prelist should be made at the earliest possible time, in order to establish accountability for the cash.
wrongWhy: Prelisting incoming cash receipts is a preventive control which is designed to prevent undesirable events from occurring, not a detective control, which is designed to expose an error or a fraud after it occurs.

Prelisting incoming cash receipts is a preventive control which is designed to prevent undesirable events from occurring, not a directive control, which is designed to ensure the occurrence of a desirable event.

Prelisting incoming cash receipts is a preventive control which is designed to prevent undesirable events from occurring, not a corrective control designed to correct undesirable events after they occur.

5-1.q: Components) Which of the following hiring procedures provides the most control over the accuracy of information submitted on an employment application?
o: The hiring organization calls the last place of employment for each finalist to verify the employment length and position held.
Applicants are required to sign that the information on the application is true and correct as a confirmation of the truth of the information in the application.
Applicants are required to submit unofficial copies of their transcripts along with the application as verification of their educational credentials.
Letters of recommendation that attest to the applicant’s character must be mailed directly to the hiring organization rather than being submitted by the applicant.
5-1.e: This represents an independent verification of employment because the hiring organization is performing the verification process.
wrongWhy: If an applicant is going to lie about information, there is no reason to believe that the applicant will not sign his or her own name to the fraudulent information. This is not an independent verification.

The applicant is providing the transcript, leading to a loss of independence. In addition, the transcript is unofficial, making it very easy to change the information and send a photocopy of the altered transcript.
There is nothing to prevent the applicants from writing the letters themselves, putting fraudulent return address information on the letters, and mailing them.

5-2.q: Residual risk is best defined as:
o: The risk before any controls are in place
The risk Remaining after management has responded to the risk with controls
The maximum possible loss from a risk event
The risk that auditors fail to detect errors
5-2.e: Residual Risk = Inherent Risk After applying controls and risk responses. If inherent risk is high and controls are strong, residual risk is low. Organizations set a target residual risk level aligned with their risk appetite. Residual risk should be monitored continuously.

5-2.q: Risk Appetite is defined as:
o: The maximum loss the company can absorb
The amount and type of risk an organization is Willing to accept to achieve its objectives — set by the board
The risk that remains after controls are applied
The risk response strategy chosen by management
5-2.e: Risk Appetite = the amount and Type of risk an organization is willing to pursue/accept in pursuit of objectives. It is set by the Board and reflects the organization's strategy and values. Risk appetite guides decisions about which risks to take and which to avoid or mitigate.

5-2.q: Which risk response strategy involves insurance or outsourcing?
o: Avoid
Reduce
Accept
Share/Transfer
5-2.e: Share/Transfer: The risk is shifted to a third party — through insurance (transfer financial impact), outsourcing (transfer operational risk), hedging (transfer financial risk), or joint ventures. The risk is not eliminated, but the financial consequences are borne by another party.

5-2.q: Enterprise Risk Management (Erm) differs from traditional internal control because it:
o: Focuses only on financial reporting risk
Also addresses Strategic risks and value creation, not just operational and compliance risks
Is only relevant for large companies
Replaces the need for internal controls
5-2.e: Erm is Broader — it addresses All risks including strategic, operational, financial, and compliance, and links risk management to Strategy and Value Creation. Traditional COSO Ic focuses on controls for financial reporting and compliance. Erm considers the entire risk portfolio and how it affects the organization's ability to achieve its mission.

5-2.q: Control risk refers to:
o: The risk that an error will occur in a transaction
The risk that the company's internal controls Fail to prevent or detect a material misstatement
The risk that auditors fail to find an error
The inherent risk in a process before controls
5-2.e: Control Risk = the risk that internal controls Fail to prevent or detect a material error or fraud. High control risk exists when controls are weak, absent, or not operating effectively. Auditors assess control risk to determine how much substantive testing is needed.

5-2.q: Inherent risk is Best defined as:
o: Risk remaining after management applies controls
Risk that auditors fail to detect errors
The level of risk that Exists Before any controls are applied
Risk related to the entity's business environment only
5-2.e: Inherent Risk = the susceptibility of an account or assertion to material misstatement Before considering any related internal controls. Some accounts are inherently riskier than others (cash is more inherently risky than prepaid expenses). Inherent risk is the Starting Point — then controls reduce it to residual risk.

5-2.q: If a company has high inherent risk and effective controls, the residual risk will be:
o: Equal to inherent risk
Higher than inherent risk
Low — strong controls significantly reduce the inherent risk
Unrelated to inherent risk
5-2.e: Residual Risk = Inherent Risk Minus the effect of controls. If inherent risk is High but controls are Strong and effective, residual risk can be Low. This is the goal of risk management: identify areas of high inherent risk and implement proportionately strong controls to bring residual risk within acceptable bounds.

5-2.q: Risk Tolerance differs from risk Appetite in that risk tolerance is:
o: Set by the board at the strategic level
The Acceptable Variation around the risk appetite — the operational range management works within
The maximum possible loss from any risk event
Identical to risk appetite in practice
5-2.e: Risk Appetite = the broad level of risk the organization will accept (set by the board). Risk Tolerance = the acceptable variation Around that appetite — the operational boundaries management uses day-to-day. Example: risk appetite = 'moderate credit risk'; risk tolerance = 'no more than 5% of receivables over 90 days.'

5-2.q: Which risk response strategy involves Eliminating the risk by not engaging in the activity at all?
o: Accept
Reduce
Share/Transfer
Avoid
5-2.e: Avoid: The organization eliminates the risk by not engaging in the risky activity. Example: a company stops offering a product line that creates significant legal liability. Most extreme and costly response — gives up potential benefits. Used when the risk is too high relative to any potential benefit.

5-2.q: Which risk response strategy is Most appropriate when the cost of mitigating a risk exceeds the expected benefit?
o: Avoid the risk entirely
Share the risk through insurance
Accept the risk — acknowledge it and do nothing (when cost > benefit)
Reduce the risk with additional controls
5-2.e: Accept (or Retain): The organization acknowledges the risk and takes no action — typically when the cost of mitigation exceeds the expected benefit, or when the residual risk is within the risk appetite. This is a conscious management decision, not neglect. It should be documented and monitored.

5-2.q: In the Audit Risk model, detection risk refers to:
o: The risk that controls fail to prevent material misstatements
The risk an account is misstated before controls
The risk that Auditors' procedures fail to find a material error that exists
The risk that management overrides controls
5-2.e: Detection Risk: The risk that Audit Procedures will fail to detect a material misstatement that exists. It is the only element of the audit risk model that auditors can Control by designing more rigorous procedures. Audit Risk = Inherent Risk × Control Risk × Detection Risk. When inherent and control risk are high, auditors lower detection risk by doing more extensive testing.

5-2.q: COSO Erm (2017) addresses which type of risk that the original COSO Ic framework does Not explicitly address?
o: Compliance risk
Operational risk
Strategic risk — risks related to achieving the organization's mission and strategy
Financial reporting risk
5-2.e: COSO Erm (2017) explicitly links risk management to Strategy and Value Creation — addressing strategic risks that could prevent the organization from achieving its mission. The original COSO Ic (2013) focuses on operations, financial reporting, and compliance but does not directly link to strategy. Erm is broader and more forward-looking.

5-2.q: Risk Threshold is Best defined as:
o: The amount of risk the board is willing to accept
The acceptable variation around risk appetite
The Level At Which Risk Becomes Unacceptable and triggers escalation or action
The maximum insurance coverage available
5-2.e: Risk Threshold = the specific level at which risk becomes unacceptable and triggers a mandatory response or escalation to senior management. It is the 'red line' beyond which management must act. Example: if customer concentration threshold = 30% revenue from one client, exceeding this triggers a mitigation plan.

5-2.q: The Governance & Culture component of COSO Erm (2017) is responsible for:
o: Identifying specific operational risks
Setting the Tone, risk appetite, and risk culture from leadership
Running day-to-day risk controls
Testing the effectiveness of risk responses
5-2.e: In COSO Erm (2017), Governance & Culture is the first component — it establishes the foundation: board oversight of Erm, organizational risk culture, the risk appetite, ethical values, and management's commitment to risk management. Without proper governance and culture, all other Erm components are ineffective.

5-2.q: A manufacturing company purchases insurance to cover potential product liability claims. This is an example of which risk response?
o: Avoid
Reduce
Share/Transfer — shifting the financial impact to the insurer
Accept
5-2.e: Share/Transfer: Insurance shifts the Financial Consequences of a risk to a third party (the insurer). The underlying risk still exists — the company still makes products that could injure people — but the financial impact is transferred. Other examples: outsourcing IT operations (transfers operational risk), currency hedging (transfers exchange rate risk).

5-2.q: Which of the following is an example of a Strategic risk?
o: An employee inputs an incorrect invoice amount
A fire destroys the warehouse
A competitor launches a superior product that makes the company's product obsolete
A vendor is paid twice for the same invoice
5-2.e: Strategic Risks threaten the organization's ability to achieve its strategic objectives. A disruptive competitor is a classic strategic risk — it affects the core business model. Incorrect invoice entry = operational risk. Fire = operational/hazard risk. Duplicate payment = operational/control risk. Erm addresses all types including strategic.

5-2.q: In Erm, the relationship between risk appetite and strategy means that:
o: Strategy is set first, then risk appetite is aligned to it
The board sets risk appetite First, and strategy choices must stay within that appetite
Risk appetite is irrelevant to strategic decisions
Risk appetite only applies after a loss event
5-2.e: In COSO Erm (2017), the Board sets the risk Appetite first (reflecting the organization's values and stakeholder expectations), then management develops Strategies that operate within that appetite. Strategies that would require accepting risk beyond the appetite must be modified or rejected. Risk appetite and strategy are tightly linked.

5-2.q: A company identifies that a new regulation will significantly increase compliance costs. This risk should First be:
o: Immediately shared through insurance
Avoided by exiting the industry
Identified And Assessed — evaluate likelihood and impact before choosing a response
Accepted without any action
5-2.e: The Erm process: Identify the risk, then Assess it (likelihood × impact), then choose a Response. Jumping to a response without assessment leads to over- or under-investment in controls. A compliance risk from new regulation may be mitigated by building internal processes, partially transferred through legal insurance, or in some cases avoided by product/market repositioning.

5-2.q: Control risk is High when:
o: Internal controls are well-designed and operating effectively
Inherent risk in the account is low
Internal Controls Are Weak or absent — they cannot prevent or detect material misstatements
The external auditor has extensive experience with the client
5-2.e: High Control Risk means internal controls are Weak or Not Functioning — they provide little protection against material misstatements. Auditors respond to high control risk by increasing substantive testing (lowering detection risk). In a well-controlled environment, control risk is low and auditors can reduce the extent of substantive testing.

5-2.q: Risk assessment in the COSO framework requires management to consider risks related to:
o: Only financial statement fraud
Both internal and external sources of risk that could affect the achievement of objectives
Only regulatory compliance
Only operational disruptions
5-2.e: COSO Risk Assessment requires management to identify and analyze risks from Both Internal sources (system failures, employee turnover, management override, process changes) and External sources (economic changes, competition, regulation, technology disruption, natural disasters). All risks that could threaten the three categories of objectives (operations, reporting, compliance) must be considered.

5-3.q: The accounting controls surrounding the revenue cycle should provide assurances of all of the following except the
o: accurate recording, shipping, and billing of all valid sales transactions.
proper authorization of all sales return and allowance transactions.
approval of all credit sales transactions after they are processed.
accurate recording of customer accounts and finished goods inventories.
5-3.e: Credit sales transactions should be approved before they are processed. That is a transaction control objective in the revenue cycle. However, there is very little purpose to approving credit sales transactions after they have been processed. At that point, if the person approving the transaction determines that the customer is not creditworthy, it is too late because the sale has already been processed and the product shipped or delivered to the customer. And there will be a high probability of a loss.
wrongWhy: Controls surrounding the revenue cycle should provide assurance of accurate recording, shipping, and billing of all valid sales transactions. Accuracy is a transaction control objective.

Controls surrounding the revenue cycle should provide assurance of proper authorization of all sales return and allowance transactions. Authorization by someone with the authority to approve the specific transactions is a transaction control objective in the revenue cycle.
Controls surrounding the revenue cycle should provide assurance of accurate recording of customer accounts and finished goods inventories. Accuracy is a transaction control objective.

5-3.q: Which one of the following would not be considered an internal control structure policy or procedure relevant to a financial statement audit?
o: Periodic reconciliation of perpetual inventory records to the general ledger control account.
Comparison of physical inventory counts to perpetual inventory records.
Timely reporting and review of quality control results.
Maintenance of control over unused checks.
5-3.e: While timely reporting and review of quality control results is important to the manufacturing process, this is not an internal control structure policy or procedure that is relevant to a financial statement audit.
wrongWhy: The periodic reconciliation of perpetual inventory records to the general ledger control account is important to ensure the accuracy of the financial statements.
The periodic comparison of physical inventory counts to perpetual inventory records is important to ensure the accuracy of the financial statements.
Maintenance of control over unused checks is a very important internal control, because it is a method of safeguarding assets.

5-3.q: The control that would most likely ensure that payroll checks are written only for authorized amounts is to:
o: Require the return of undelivered checks to the cashier.
Conduct periodic floor verification of employees on the payroll.
Require supervisory approval of employee timecards.
Periodically witness the distribution of payroll checks.
5-3.e: The employee’s supervisor would be in the best position to ensure payment of the proper amount.
wrongWhy: Undelivered checks provide no evidence regarding the validity of the amounts.
Employees may be properly included on payroll, but the amounts paid may be unauthorized.
Witnessing a payroll distribution would not assure that amounts paid are authorized.

5-3.q: The procedure that would best discourage the resubmission of vendor invoices after they have been paid is
o: The cancellation of vouchers by accounting personnel.
The mailing of payments directly to payees by accounting personnel.
The cancellation of vouchers by treasurer personnel.
A requirement for double endorsement of checks.
5-3.e: A voucher is an internal accounting document that is usually created after the company has received an invoice from a vendor or service provider and has successfully matched the invoice to a purchase order. In other words, it is created after the company has determined that this invoice is genuine and should be paid. The voucher communicates the company's intent to make the payment to the vendor or service provider. Among other things, the voucher contains detailed information about the transaction, including the payee, the amount of the payment to be made, and a description of the transaction. Based on the authorization in the voucher, the check is then created to pay the invoice. The accounting system will show that a voucher has been created for the purchase order issued for that transaction and that it has been cancelled; and a second voucher cannot be created. So if a voucher is cancelled after the check has been written and signed, payment cannot be made a second time even if the vendor does submit a duplicate invoice, because there is no active voucher to authorize a second payment. Therefore, the voucher and all supporting documents should be cancelled by personnel in the treasurer's office at the time the check is signed.
wrongWhy: Cancellation of vouchers should not take place until the check in payment of the invoice has been signed.
The account payable personnel should not have access to checks after they have been signed.
Requiring two signatures on a check would not prevent resubmission and double paying of vendor invoices after they have been paid.

5-3.q: Certain limitations are inherent in any internal control system. Which one of the following scenarios is the result of an inherent limitation of internal control?
o: A security guard allows one of the warehouse employees to remove company assets from the premises without authorization.
The firm sells to customers on account, without credit approval.
The comptroller both makes and records cash deposits.
An employee, who is unable to read, is assigned custody of the firm's computer tape library and run manuals that are used during the third shift.
5-3.e: If two employees (the security guard and the warehouse employee) collude to defraud their employer, a control based on segregation of functions can be rendered ineffective. This is an inherent limitation of internal control.
wrongWhy: This is not an inherent limitation of internal control, because it could and should be avoided through adequate credit approval of sales.
This is not an inherent limitation of internal control, because it could and should be avoided through adequate segregation of duties.

Assignment of an employee who is unable to read to a job requiring reading is avoidable through adequate testing of potential employees. Therefore, it is not an inherent limitation of internal control.

5-3.q: Auditors document their understanding of internal control with questionnaires, flowcharts, and narrative descriptions. A questionnaire consists of a series of questions concerning controls that auditors consider necessary to prevent or detect errors and irregularities. The most appropriate question designed to contribute to the auditors' understanding of the completeness of the expenditure cycle would concern the
o: Disposition of cash receipts.
Qualifications of accounting personnel.
Internal verification of quantities, prices, and mathematical accuracy of sales invoices.
Use and accountability of prenumbered checks.
5-3.e: In understanding the completeness of the expenditure cycle, the auditor is interested in whether all the transactions have been recorded. If prenumbered checks are used sequentially, a gap in check numbers would be something for the auditor to investigate, because it may mean that there are unrecorded transactions.
wrongWhy: Cash receipts are unrelated to the expenditure cycle and can contribute nothing to the auditors' understanding of the completeness of the expenditure cycle.
Qualifications of accounting personnel are unrelated to the controls over the expenditure cycle.
Verification of sales invoices will not contribute to an understanding of the completeness of the expenditure cycle, because sales invoices are part of the revenue cycle.

5-3.q: The proper segregation of duties requires that:
o: The individual who records a transaction must maintain custody of the asset resulting from the transaction.
The individual who records a transaction does not compare the accounting record of the asset with the asset itself.
The individual who maintains custody of an asset must have access to the accounting records for the asset.
The individual who authorizes a transaction also records it.
5-3.e: Segregation of duties requires that the following four functions be done by different people: (1) authorizing a transaction; (2) recording the transaction; (3) keeping physical custody of the related asset; and (4) periodic reconciliation of the physical asset with the recorded amount for the asset. If the individual recording the transaction were to also compare the accounting record of the asset with the asset itself, that person would be performing two of the above functions: recording the transaction and periodic reconciliation of the physical asset with the recorded amount for the asset.
wrongWhy: This is not a requirement of the proper segregation of duties.
This is not a requirement of the proper segregation of duties.
This is not a requirement of the proper segregation of duties.

5-3.q: ABC is a major retailer with over 52 department stores. The marketing department is responsible for: Conducting marketing surveys. Recommending locations for new store openings. Ordering products and determining retail prices for the products. Developing promotion and advertising for each line of products. Determining the pricing of special sale items. The marketing department has separate product managers for each product line. Each product manager is given a purchasing budget by the marketing manager. Product managers are not rotated among product lines because of the need to acquire product knowledge and to build relationships with vendors. A subsection of the department does marketing surveys. In addition to ordering and pricing, the product managers also determine the timing and method of product delivery. Products are delivered to a central distribution center where goods are received, retail prices are marked on the product, and the goods are segregated for distribution to stores. Receiving documents are created by scanning in receipts; the number of items scanned in are reconciled with the price tags generated and attached to products. The average product spends between 12 and 72 hours in the distribution center before being loaded on trucks for delivery to each store. Receipts are recorded at the distribution center, thus the company has not found the need to maintain a receiving function at each store. Each product manager is evaluated on a combination of sales and gross profit generated from their product line. Many products are seasonal and individual store managers can require that seasonal products be "cleared out" to make space for the next season's products. Requests for purchases beyond those initially budgeted by the marketing manager must be approved by the marketing manager. Which of the following statements regarding this control procedure is correct? The procedure I. should provide for the most efficient allocation of scarce organizational resources. II. is a detective control procedure. III. is not necessary because each product manager is evaluated on profit generated, and thus this control is redundant.
o: I only.
III only.
II and III.
I, II, and III.
5-3.e: The company must keep purchases within the limits of available financing, and it must allocate its limited warehouse and display space among its various merchandise lines. Having the marketing manager approve additional purchases beyond those initially budgeted provides a means to allocate the company's resources in order to maximize the organization's total return.
wrongWhy: The company must keep purchases within the limits of available financing, and it must allocate its limited warehouse and display space among its various merchandise lines. This control is not redundant because the evaluation of managers on profit generated does nothing to control the allocation of these two resources.

Approval of requests for purchases beyond those initially budgeted by the marketing manager is not a detective control; it is a preventive control. The company must keep purchases within the limits of available financing, and it must allocate its limited warehouse and display space among its various merchandise lines. This control is not redundant because the evaluation of managers on profit generated does nothing to control the allocation of these two resources.

Approval of requests for purchases beyond those initially budgeted by the marketing manager is not a detective control; it is a preventive control. The company must keep purchases within the limits of available financing, and it must allocate its limited warehouse and display space among its various merchandise lines. This control is not redundant because the evaluation of managers on profit generated does nothing to control the allocation of these two resources.

5-3.q: Control risk is the risk that a material misstatement in an account will not be prevented or detected on a timely basis by the client's internal control structure policies or procedures. The best control procedure to prevent or detect fictitious payroll transactions is
o: Periodic independent bank reconciliations of the payroll bank account.
Personnel department authorization for hiring, pay rate, job status, and termination.
To use and account for prenumbered payroll checks.
Internal verification of authorized pay rates, computations, and agreement with the payroll register.
5-3.e: The personnel department should authorize all hiring, pay rates, job status changes, and terminations. This is the authorization function, and it should be separate from the recording function, which is performed by the payroll department.
wrongWhy: Reconciliations of the payroll bank account would not prevent or detect fictitious payroll transactions.

The use of pre-numbered payroll checks is a control procedure that ensures that all checks are accounted for in the accounting records. However, it would not prevent or detect fictitious payroll transactions.
Although these are important controls, they would not prevent or detect fictitious payroll transactions.

5-3.q: Management wishes to include in its internal controls over factory payroll a procedure to ensure that employees are paid only for work actually performed. To meet this objective, which of the following internal control actions would be most appropriate?
o: Have supervisors distribute paychecks to employees in their sections.
Use time cards.
Keep unclaimed paychecks in a vault.
Compare piecework records with inventory additions from production.
5-3.e: Comparing piecework records with inventory additions is a cross-check on factory production performed. If employees are being paid on a piece-work basis, this could be used to ensure that payments are made only for work performed.
wrongWhy: Having supervisors distribute paychecks to their employees will not ensure that employees are paid only for work actually performed.
A time card may be falsified by having another employee punch it.

Keeping unclaimed paychecks in a vault will not ensure that employees are paid only for work actually performed. Furthermore, if the paychecks are legitimate and are truly unclaimed, most states have escheat laws that require unclaimed property to be turned over the state after a period of time.

5-3.q: Which of the following controls would be the most appropriate means to ensure that terminated employees had been removed from the payroll?
o: Mailing checks to employees' residences.
Establishing computerized limit checks on payroll rates.
Establishing direct-deposit procedures with employees' banks.
Reconciling payroll and time-keeping records.
5-3.e: If an employee has been terminated but the employee has not been removed from the payroll, a reconciliation of payroll records with time-keeping records should detect it.
wrongWhy: Mailing checks to employees' homes does nothing to verify whether all the paychecks are valid. Using this procedure, terminated employees who had not been removed from the payroll would continue to receive paychecks.
This procedure would detect excessive pay to current employees but not inappropriate pay to terminated employees.

Establishing direct-deposit procedures with employees' banks does nothing to verify whether all the paychecks are valid. Using this procedure, terminated employees who had not been removed from the payroll would continue to receive paychecks.

5-3.q: The Computer Center of a company processes its prior week's sales invoices, as well as its returns and allowances, at the end of the week. Cash receipts, however, are processed and deposited daily. Each morning the mail receipts clerk prepares the cash receipts prelist in duplicate. The original prelist goes to the head cashier together with the checks and an adding machine tape. The duplicate copy goes to the accounts receivable supervisor. The separate remittance advices are sent to the data input clerk. At midday, the head cashier prepares the bank deposit slip which is taken to the bank. After returning from the bank, the head cashier compares the original prelist to the validated bank deposit slip, initials the documents, and files them in chronological order. The following morning the accounts receivable supervisor receives a summary processing list from the Computer Center with various control totals from the nightly accounts receivable update. The total on the prior day's duplicate cash receipts prelist is then compared with the total showing the difference between the prior day's beginning and ending accounts receivable subsidiary ledger totals. The amount shown on yesterday's duplicate cash receipts prelist was $35,532.32. This morning the difference between the beginning and ending subsidiary ledger totals was $35,541.32. What is the first thing that the accounts receivable supervisor should do to try to resolve the discrepancy in the two amounts?
o: Call the head cashier to determine the amount deposited.
Manually recalculate the total on the cash receipts prelist.
Compare the accounts receivable subsidiary ledger total with the total in the accounts receivable general ledger account.
Send a copy of the prelist and the Summary Processing List to the Internal Audit Department.
5-3.e: This takes a short period of time and includes external verification of the amount on the cash receipts prelist. This would prove that an error was made during data input suggesting that further investigative effort should be concentrated there.
wrongWhy: External validation of the total amount deposited is a better way of verifying the total cash receipts, as well as more efficient.
The narrative implies that the AR General Ledger Account is updated when the AR subsidiary ledgers are updated. Thus, there would be no difference between these amounts.
Minor errors should be investigated and corrected by operating personnel.

5-3.q: The Computer Center of a company processes its prior week's sales invoices, as well as its returns and allowances, at the end of the week. Cash receipts, however, are processed and deposited daily. Each morning the mail receipts clerk prepares the cash receipts prelist in duplicate. The original prelist goes to the head cashier together with the checks and an adding machine tape. The duplicate copy goes to the accounts receivable supervisor. The separate remittance advices are sent to the data input clerk. At midday, the head cashier prepares the bank deposit slip which is taken to the bank. After returning from the bank, the head cashier compares the original prelist to the validated bank deposit slip, initials the documents, and files them in chronological order. The following morning the accounts receivable supervisor receives a summary processing list from the Computer Center with various control totals from the nightly accounts receivable update. The total on the prior day's duplicate cash receipts prelist is then compared with the total showing the difference between the prior day's beginning and ending accounts receivable subsidiary ledger totals. The amount shown on yesterday's duplicate cash receipts prelist was $35,532.32. This morning the difference between the beginning and ending subsidiary ledger totals was $35,541.32. What is the most likely reason for the difference between the two amounts?
o: A transposition error occurred.
The total on the cash receipts prelist was miscalculated.
A remittance advice was recorded twice.
An irregularity occurred during data output.
5-3.e: The amount of a discrepancy between two batch totals often provides a clue about the error. When a difference can be divided evenly by 9, a transposition error may have occurred during data input where the column amounts in two adjacent columns are exchanged. Other possibilities to consider include: looking for transactions exactly equal to the amount of the discrepancy or transactions equal to half of the discrepancy. In the latter case, a transaction may have been incorrectly debited or credited.
wrongWhy: If the prior day's cash receipts prelist were wrong, the Head Cashier is likely to have discovered and reported this either when preparing the bank deposit or after agreeing the prelist to the validated bank deposit.
The nature of the discrepancy, a small number in an even dollar amount evenly divisible by 9, suggests that double recording is less likely than a transposition error.
If an error or irregularity had occurred, it would have been likely during data input, not output.

5-3.q: Marport Company is a manufacturing company that uses forms and documents in its accounting information systems for record keeping and internal control. The departments in Marport's organization structure and their primary responsibilities are: Accounts Payable -- authorize payments and prepare vouchers. Accounts Receivable -- maintain customer accounts. Billing -- prepare invoices to customers for goods sold. Cashier -- maintain a record of cash receipts and disbursements. Credit Department -- verify the credit rating of customers. Cost Accounting -- accumulate manufacturing costs for all goods produced. Finished Goods Storeroom -- maintain the physical inventory and related stock records of finished goods. General Accounting -- maintain all records for the company's general ledger. Internal Audit -- appraise and monitor internal controls, as well as conduct operational and management audits. Inventory Control -- maintain perpetual inventory records for all manufacturing materials and supplies. Mailroom -- process incoming, outgoing, and interdepartmental mail. Payroll -- compute and prepare the company payroll. Personnel -- hire employees, as well as maintain records on job positions and employees. Purchasing -- place orders for materials and supplies. Production -- manufacture finished goods. Production Planning -- decide the types and quantities of products to be produced. Receiving -- receive all materials and supplies. Sales -- accept orders from customers. Shipping -- ship goods to customers. Stores Control -- safeguard all materials and supplies until needed for production. Timekeeping -- prepare and control time worked by hourly employees. The document that is the authorization to initiate the manufacture of goods is referred to as a
o: Raw materials requisition.
Bill of materials.
Production order.
Daily production schedule.
5-3.e: The Production Planning Department would use a Production Order to authorize the Production Department to manufacture certain items.
wrongWhy: The Production Department would request raw materials by means of a raw materials requisition, but the raw materials requisition would not authorize the initiation of manufacturing.
A bill of materials is the list of component parts that go into the manufacture of each item of finished goods. It is not an authorization to initiate manufacturing.
The daily production schedule is used for production planning. It is not an authorization to initiate manufacturing.

5-3.q: Marport Company is a manufacturing company that uses forms and documents in its accounting information systems for record keeping and internal control. The departments in Marport's organization structure and their primary responsibilities are: Accounts Payable -- authorize payments and prepare vouchers. Accounts Receivable -- maintain customer accounts. Billing -- prepare invoices to customers for goods sold. Cashier -- maintain a record of cash receipts and disbursements. Credit Department -- verify the credit rating of customers. Cost Accounting -- accumulate manufacturing costs for all goods produced. Finished Goods Storeroom -- maintain the physical inventory and related stock records of finished goods. General Accounting -- maintain all records for the company's general ledger. Internal Audit -- appraise and monitor internal controls, as well as conduct operational and management audits. Inventory Control -- maintain perpetual inventory records for all manufacturing materials and supplies. Mailroom -- process incoming, outgoing, and interdepartmental mail. Payroll -- compute and prepare the company payroll. Personnel -- hire employees, as well as maintain records on job positions and employees. Purchasing -- place orders for materials and supplies. Production -- manufacture finished goods. Production Planning -- decide the types and quantities of products to be produced. Receiving -- receive all materials and supplies. Sales -- accept orders from customers. Shipping -- ship goods to customers. Stores Control -- safeguard all materials and supplies until needed for production. Timekeeping -- prepare and control time worked by hourly employees. If employee paychecks are distributed by hand to employees, which one of the following departments should be responsible for the safekeeping of unclaimed paychecks?
o: Cashier Department.
Personnel Department.
Payroll Department.
Production Department in which the employee works or worked.
5-3.e: The Cashier Department is a part of the treasury function, which should properly be responsible for a custodial function such as safekeeping of unclaimed checks.
wrongWhy: The Personnel Department fulfills what is in part an authorization function as it is responsible for pay rates. Thus, it should not perform what is primarily a custodial function as well.
The Payroll Department fulfills the recordkeeping function. Thus, it should not perform what is primarily a custodial function as well.
An employee or supervisor in the department in which the employee works or worked could misappropriate an unclaimed check.

5-3.q: Marport Company is a manufacturing company that uses forms and documents in its accounting information systems for record keeping and internal control. The departments in Marport's organization structure and their primary responsibilities are: Accounts Payable -- authorize payments and prepare vouchers. Accounts Receivable -- maintain customer accounts. Billing -- prepare invoices to customers for goods sold. Cashier -- maintain a record of cash receipts and disbursements. Credit Department -- verify the credit rating of customers. Cost Accounting -- accumulate manufacturing costs for all goods produced. Finished Goods Storeroom -- maintain the physical inventory and related stock records of finished goods. General Accounting -- maintain all records for the company's general ledger. Internal Audit -- appraise and monitor internal controls, as well as conduct operational and management audits. Inventory Control -- maintain perpetual inventory records for all manufacturing materials and supplies. Mailroom -- process incoming, outgoing, and interdepartmental mail. Payroll -- compute and prepare the company payroll. Personnel -- hire employees, as well as maintain records on job positions and employees. Purchasing -- place orders for materials and supplies. Production -- manufacture finished goods. Production Planning -- decide the types and quantities of products to be produced. Receiving -- receive all materials and supplies. Sales -- accept orders from customers. Shipping -- ship goods to customers. Stores Control -- safeguard all materials and supplies until needed for production. Timekeeping -- prepare and control time worked by hourly employees. Responsibility for following up on any problems regarding orders of production materials and supplies, such as orders for which no acknowledgment has been received, orders overdue, partial orders, damaged or substandard merchandise received on an order, etc., would be entrusted to the
o: Purchasing Department.
Stores Control Department.
Receiving Department.
Production Planning Department.
5-3.e: The Purchasing Department is responsible for following up on any problems regarding orders of production materials and supplies.
wrongWhy: The Stores Department is responsible for safeguarding all materials and supplies after they are received and until they are needed for production.
The receiving department receives the order, but after it has been received and recorded, they do not have responsibility for following up on problems regarding the orders.
The Production Planning Department is responsible for the manufacturing. They are not responsible for following up on problems with purchase orders.

5-3.q: Marport Company is a manufacturing company that uses forms and documents in its accounting information systems for record keeping and internal control. The departments in Marport's organization structure and their primary responsibilities are: Accounts Payable -- authorize payments and prepare vouchers. Accounts Receivable -- maintain customer accounts. Billing -- prepare invoices to customers for goods sold. Cashier -- maintain a record of cash receipts and disbursements. Credit Department -- verify the credit rating of customers. Cost Accounting -- accumulate manufacturing costs for all goods produced. Finished Goods Storeroom -- maintain the physical inventory and related stock records of finished goods. General Accounting -- maintain all records for the company's general ledger. Internal Audit -- appraise and monitor internal controls, as well as conduct operational and management audits. Inventory Control -- maintain perpetual inventory records for all manufacturing materials and supplies. Mailroom -- process incoming, outgoing, and interdepartmental mail. Payroll -- compute and prepare the company payroll. Personnel -- hire employees, as well as maintain records on job positions and employees. Purchasing -- place orders for materials and supplies. Production -- manufacture finished goods. Production Planning -- decide the types and quantities of products to be produced. Receiving -- receive all materials and supplies. Sales -- accept orders from customers. Shipping -- ship goods to customers. Stores Control -- safeguard all materials and supplies until needed for production. Timekeeping -- prepare and control time worked by hourly employees. Multiple copies of each purchase order are prepared for record keeping and distribution with a copy of the purchase order sent to the vendor and one retained by the Purchasing Department. In addition, for proper informational flow and internal control purposes, a version of the purchase order would be distributed to the
o: Accounts Payable, Receiving, and Stores Control Departments.
Accounts Payable, Accounts Receivable, and Receiving Departments.
Accounts Payable, Receiving, and Inventory Control Departments.
Accounts Payable, Receiving, and Production Planning Departments.
