5-3.e: The Accounts Payable Department needs copies of purchase orders to check the vendor's invoice; the Receiving Department needs copies of purchase orders with quantities omitted, so the count of items received should be honest; and the Inventory Control Department needs copies of purchase orders in order to know what orders have been placed.
wrongWhy: The Stores Control Department does not need copies of purchase orders.
The Accounts Receivable Department does not have a need to receive copies of purchase orders.
The Production Planning Department does not have a need to receive copies of purchase orders.

5-3.q: Marport Company is a manufacturing company that uses forms and documents in its accounting information systems for record keeping and internal control. The departments in Marport's organization structure and their primary responsibilities are: Accounts Payable -- authorize payments and prepare vouchers. Accounts Receivable -- maintain customer accounts. Billing -- prepare invoices to customers for goods sold. Cashier -- maintain a record of cash receipts and disbursements. Credit Department -- verify the credit rating of customers. Cost Accounting -- accumulate manufacturing costs for all goods produced. Finished Goods Storeroom -- maintain the physical inventory and related stock records of finished goods. General Accounting -- maintain all records for the company's general ledger. Internal Audit -- appraise and monitor internal controls, as well as conduct operational and management audits. Inventory Control -- maintain perpetual inventory records for all manufacturing materials and supplies. Mailroom -- process incoming, outgoing, and interdepartmental mail. Payroll -- compute and prepare the company payroll. Personnel -- hire employees, as well as maintain records on job positions and employees. Purchasing -- place orders for materials and supplies. Production -- manufacture finished goods. Production Planning -- decide the types and quantities of products to be produced. Receiving -- receive all materials and supplies. Sales -- accept orders from customers. Shipping -- ship goods to customers. Stores Control -- safeguard all materials and supplies until needed for production. Timekeeping -- prepare and control time worked by hourly employees. The initiation of the purchase of materials and supplies would be the responsibility of the
o: Purchasing Department.
Stores Control Department.
Production Department.
Inventory Control Department.
5-3.e: The Inventory Control Department maintains perpetual inventory records for all manufacturing materials and supplies. Therefore, it would be in a position to know when supplies are getting low and would be responsible for initiating a purchase requisition.
wrongWhy: The Purchasing Department places orders, but they do not initiate them. Orders are initiated and authorized by others.
Stores Control safeguards the materials and supplies until they are needed for production. They do not maintain inventory records or initiate purchase requisitions.
The Production Department manufactures the goods, obtaining its materials from the Stores Control Department. It does not initiate requests for purchases.

5-3.q: Which one of the following duties assigned to the cashier exposes the organization to a control risk in terms of segregation of duties?
o: Signing the deposit slips.
Depositing cash at the bank.
Comparing the check register with the bank statement.
Entering information in the accounts receivable subsidiary ledger.
5-3.e: An individual who has access to the asset, in this case, cash, should not also have access to the records of that asset. This situation represents a control risk in terms of the segregation of duties.
wrongWhy: The cashier signing the deposit slips does not represent a control risk in terms of segregation of duties.
The cashier depositing cash at the bank does not represent a control risk in terms of segregation of duties.
The cashier comparing the check register with the bank statement does not represent a control risk in terms of segregation of duties.

5-3.q: A company has computerized sales and cash receipts journals. The computer programs for these journals have been properly debugged. The auditor discovered that the total of the accounts receivable subsidiary accounts differs materially from the accounts receivable control account. This could indicate
o: Statements being intercepted prior to mailing.
Receivables not being properly aged.
Lapping of receivables.
Credit memoranda being improperly recorded.
5-3.e: If subsidiary accounts are being credited for returns but the general ledger account is not being credited, this would cause material differences between the total of the accounts receivable subsidiary accounts and the accounts receivable control account. This can occur easily if an incorrect procedure is being used to record returns. The auditor should query the people who process the credits to customers' accounts to find out what procedure is being used and should investigate what accounting entries result from that procedure.
wrongWhy: Interception of customer statements might be a sign that fraud is taking place, but it would not cause the subsidiary accounts to not reconcile with the control account.
If receivables are being aged improperly, this would not affect customer balances or the general ledger control account balance.

Lapping of receivables would not result in a difference between the subsidiary accounts and the general ledger control account. Lapping of receivables occurs when an employee pockets a payment received on one customer's account and then applies a payment made by another customer to the first customer's account, and on and on. If that is occurring, the total of the subsidiary accounts will reconcile with the general ledger control account, but they will both be incorrect because of the theft.

5-4.q: General IT Controls (Gitc) differ from application controls because Gitc:
o: Apply only to payroll systems
Apply to the Overall IT Environment and support all application controls
Are only tested during year-end audits
Control only data input validation
5-4.e: Gitc (General IT Controls) apply to the Overall IT environment — they are the foundation for application controls. If Gitc are weak (e.g., poor access controls, no change management), application controls built on top may also be unreliable. Examples: logical access security, change management, backup procedures.

5-4.q: Which control is designed to ensure only Authorized users access the accounting system?
o: Output controls
Batch totals
Logical access controls (user IDs, passwords, role-based access)
Run-to-run totals
5-4.e: Logical Access Controls restrict system access to authorized users. These include user IDs, strong passwords, multi-factor authentication, and role-based access (users can only access what their job requires). This is a fundamental preventive IT control aligned with the Confidentiality principle of the CIA triad.

5-4.q: The CIA Triad in information security stands for:
o: Control, Integrity, Authorization
Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
Compliance, Investigation, Assurance
Custody, Independence, Accountability
5-4.e: CIA Triad: (1) Confidentiality — data accessible only to authorized users (controls: encryption, access restrictions); (2) Integrity — data is accurate and not tampered with (controls: audit trails, hash functions); (3) Availability — systems accessible when needed (controls: backups, redundancy, disaster recovery). All three must be balanced.

5-4.q: In IT segregation of duties, who should Not also be the computer operator?
o: Data entry clerk
The external auditor
The Programmer — writing code and operating the system creates incompatible duties
The internal auditor
5-4.e: The Programmer and the Computer Operator should be Separated. If the same person writes/modifies code And operates the system day-to-day, they could make unauthorized program changes and then process transactions to cover fraud. Key IT Sod separations: Analyst ≠ Programmer ≠ Operator ≠ Data Entry.

5-4.q: An Input control in an accounting application that rejects a transaction if the date is more than 30 days old is an example of a:
o: Batch total control
Validity / Reasonableness Check — prevents processing of illogical data
Output distribution control
Change management control
5-4.e: Validity / Reasonableness Check is an application input control that tests whether data is logically valid (e.g., date within acceptable range, amount within expected limits). It prevents processing of stale, erroneous, or fraudulent input. Other input controls: format checks (field type), completeness checks (required fields), range checks (amounts within bounds).

5-4.q: Change management controls in IT are designed to:
o: Allow programmers to make system updates at any time
Ensure that changes to programs and systems are authorized, tested, and documented before implementation
Monitor user access to financial data
Back up data on a regular schedule
5-4.e: Change Management Controls govern the entire software development/change lifecycle: changes must be (1) requested and Authorized; (2) tested in a non-production environment; (3) reviewed for security impacts; (4) documented; (5) approved before promotion to production. Without these controls, unauthorized or untested code changes can introduce errors, backdoors, or fraud mechanisms.

5-4.q: Role-Based Access Control (Rbac) assigns system access based on:
o: The employee's seniority and years of service
The employee's Job Function — users receive only the access needed for their specific role
The employee's personal preferences
A fixed access level for all employees in a department
5-4.e: Rbac implements the Least Privilege principle — users get Only the access their job requires, no more. An accounts payable clerk should access AP functions only, not payroll or general ledger setup. This limits the damage a compromised account or dishonest employee can cause and implements IT segregation of duties automatically through system permissions.

5-4.q: A Batch Total is an example of which type of IT application control?
o: Output control
Processing Control — verifying that all records in a batch are processed correctly
Input control — validating data before entry
Change management control
5-4.e: Batch Totals are Processing Controls: before processing, a control total (sum of amounts, count of records) is calculated. After processing, the same total is recalculated. If they don't match, records were lost, duplicated, or altered during processing. Also called run-to-run totals or hash totals. They ensure the Completeness and Accuracy of processing.

5-4.q: Under the Shared Responsibility Model in cloud computing, the cloud Provider is responsible for:
o: Securing the customer's data and applications
Securing The Underlying Infrastructure — hardware, hypervisor, physical facilities, and network
Setting the customer's access permissions
Encrypting the customer's sensitive data
5-4.e: In the Shared Responsibility Model: the Cloud Provider secures the physical infrastructure, hypervisors, and global network. The Customer is responsible for data security, application security, access management, and encryption of their own data. This division varies by service type (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS). Many cloud data breaches occur because the customer fails to properly secure Their side of the model.

5-4.q: Encryption primarily protects which element of the CIA Triad?
o: Availability — systems are accessible when needed
Integrity — data has not been tampered with
Confidentiality — only authorized users can read the encrypted data
All three equally
5-4.e: Encryption primarily protects Confidentiality: it transforms data into unreadable ciphertext so only those with the decryption key can read it. If encrypted data is intercepted (e.g., in transit on a network), it is useless to the attacker. Hash functions and digital signatures protect Integrity. Redundancy and backups protect Availability.

5-4.q: An Audit Trail in an accounting system primarily protects which element of the CIA Triad?
o: Confidentiality — restricts who can see data
Availability — ensures systems are accessible
Integrity — provides a record of who changed what data and when
Performance — speeds up transaction processing
5-4.e: Audit Trails protect Integrity — they record every change to data: who made it, when, what the before and after values were. This deters unauthorized changes (people know there's a record) and enables detection and investigation of tampering. Audit trails are a critical IT control for financial systems where data integrity is paramount.

5-4.q: Multi-Factor Authentication (Mfa) strengthens access control by requiring:
o: A very long password of at least 16 characters
Two Or More Verification Factors from different categories: something you know + something you have + something you are
All employees to share a single login credential
Daily password changes
5-4.e: Mfa requires verification from multiple categories: (1) Something You Know — password/Pin; (2) Something You Have — phone/token/smart card; (3) Something You Are — fingerprint/facial recognition. Even if a password is stolen, the attacker cannot access the system without the second factor. Mfa significantly reduces the risk of unauthorized access from phishing or credential theft.

5-4.q: A Soc 2 report provides assurance about a Service Organization'S controls related to:
o: Financial statement accuracy only
Tax compliance
Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and/or Privacy of systems
The service organization's revenue recognition policies
5-4.e: Soc 2 (System and Organization Controls 2) reports provide third-party assurance on a service organization's controls related to the Trust Service Criteria: Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy. User entities (customers) rely on Soc 2 reports to assess the controls of cloud providers, SaaS vendors, and other IT service providers without conducting their own audits.

5-4.q: Output controls in an IT system are designed to ensure that:
o: Only valid data is entered into the system
All records are processed completely and accurately
Reports And Output Are Complete, Accurate, And Distributed Only To Authorized Recipients
Programs are tested before deployment
5-4.e: Output Controls ensure the final products of processing: (1) Completeness — all records are included in output; (2) Accuracy — output correctly reflects the processed data; (3) Distribution — reports go only to authorized people who need them; (4) Retention — output is kept for the required period. Weak output controls can lead to sensitive information reaching unauthorized people.

5-4.q: A Firewall primarily protects against which IT threat?
o: Internal employee data entry errors
Data Loss after hardware failure
Unauthorized External Network Access — filtering traffic between the network and the internet
Corruption of data by authorized users
5-4.e: A Firewall is a network security device that Monitors and Controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on security rules. It creates a barrier between a trusted internal network and untrusted external networks (internet), blocking unauthorized access attempts while allowing legitimate traffic. Firewalls primarily protect Confidentiality and network security, not internal data entry errors.

5-4.q: In IT segregation of duties, which two roles are Most Important to separate?
o: Data entry and report generation
Systems analyst and data entry
Programmer and Computer Operator — to prevent unauthorized code changes that manipulate transactions
Network administrator and database administrator
5-4.e: Programmer + Computer Operator is the most critical IT Sod separation. A programmer with operational access could: write fraudulent code And execute it in production And process transactions. This complete control enables systematic fraud that is difficult to detect. The analyst designs, the programmer codes, the operator runs — Separate people for each function.

5-4.q: A Range Check as an input control would reject a transaction if:
o: The transaction date is in the wrong format
A required field is left blank
The amount entered is outside the Expected Acceptable Range (e.g., negative quantity or impossibly large amount)
The user does not have the required access level
5-4.e: Range Checks test whether numeric input falls within predefined acceptable boundaries. Example: a quantity field that accepts only 1–9,999; a date that must be within the current fiscal year; a discount rate that cannot exceed 50%. They prevent obviously incorrect or fraudulent values from entering the system. Format check = structure. Completeness check = required fields. Access control = authorization.

5-4.q: Backup and recovery procedures Primarily protect which element of the CIA Triad?
o: Confidentiality — who can see the data
Integrity — accuracy of data
Availability — ensuring data and systems can be restored and accessed when needed
Performance — speed of data access
5-4.e: Backups primarily protect Availability — if data is lost due to hardware failure, ransomware, or disaster, backups enable Restoration of systems and data. Without adequate backups, even a minor disaster could result in permanent data loss. Backup procedures should be tested regularly (restore tests) to confirm they actually work when needed.

5-4.q: The Primary purpose of Logical Access Controls over a financial application is to:
o: Speed up transaction processing
Restrict Access to the system to only Authorized users and ensure they can only perform functions their job requires
Prevent physical theft of computers
Test the accuracy of accounting calculations
5-4.e: Logical Access Controls (passwords, Mfa, role-based access, user IDs) are the primary IT control ensuring only Authorized users access financial systems and that their access is Limited to what their job requires (least privilege). They implement IT segregation of duties by system design and are the foundation of IT security for financial applications.

5-4.q: Intrusion Detection Systems (Ids) in IT security are an example of which type of control?
o: Preventive — they stop attacks before they happen
Detective — they Identify and alert on suspicious or malicious network activity as it occurs
Corrective — they fix damage caused by attacks
Authorization — they approve network traffic
5-4.e: Ids are Detective controls — they Monitor network or system activity for suspicious patterns and Alert security staff when potential intrusions are detected. They do not block attacks (that is a firewall/Ips function) — they identify them for response. Intrusion Prevention Systems (Ips) actively block suspicious traffic and are more preventive in nature.

5-4.q: Cobit (Control Objectives for Information and Related Technologies) is Best described as:
o: A US government regulation for cybersecurity
A set of accounting standards for software companies
An IT Governance Framework developed by Isaca that aligns IT with business objectives and manages IT risks
A replacement for the COSO internal control framework
5-4.e: Cobit is an IT Governance framework developed by Isaca. It provides a comprehensive framework for enterprise IT governance and management — ensuring IT supports business strategy, resources are used responsibly, and IT risks are managed. Cobit is Complementary to COSO (not a replacement) — COSO covers the entire organization; Cobit extends that specifically for IT governance and control.

5-4.q: Cobit and COSO are Best described as:
o: Competing frameworks — organizations must choose one or the other
Identical frameworks with different names
Complementary frameworks — COSO provides the organizational internal control foundation; Cobit extends it specifically for IT governance
Government regulations that replace professional judgment
5-4.e: Cobit and COSO are Complementary: COSO provides the broad organizational internal control framework (5 components, 17 principles) covering operations, financial reporting, and compliance. Cobit maps to COSO and extends it specifically for the IT governance environment — providing detailed control objectives for IT strategy, acquisition, delivery, and monitoring. Organizations use both: COSO as the foundation, Cobit for IT-specific governance.

5-4.q: The Primary distinction between Cobit and Itil is that Cobit focuses on:
o: How to deliver IT services to end users
Day-to-day IT operations management
IT Governance And Control — what IT should accomplish and how it is governed; Itil focuses on how IT services are delivered
Hardware and network infrastructure standards
5-4.e: Cobit vs Itil: Cobit = IT Governance — addresses What IT should do: how IT aligns with business strategy, how IT risks are managed, how IT investments are governed. Itil (IT Infrastructure Library) = IT Service Management — addresses How IT services are delivered and operated day-to-day. Organizations use both: Cobit for governance, Itil for service delivery operations.

5-5.q: Sarbanes-Oxley Act) Control deficiencies can be classified in various ways. When a control is needed to meet a control objective but the control is missing, that is classified as a
o: Deficiency of omission.
deficiency in design.
effective deficiency.
deficiency in operation.
5-5.e: When a control that is needed in order to meet a control objective is missing or an existing control is not properly designed so that even if the control operates as designed, the control objective would not be met, that is a deficiency in design.
wrongWhy: Deficiency of omission is not a classification used for control deficiencies.
Effective deficiency is not a classification used for control deficiencies.

When a control that is needed in order to meet the control objective is missing, that is not a deficiency in operation. A deficiency in operation exists when a properly designed control does not operate as designed, or if the person performing the control does not possess the necessary authority or qualifications to perform the control effectively.

5-5.q: Sarbanes-Oxley Act) Four types of tests are usually used to test the adequacy of internal controls. The least reliable testing method is
o: Inspection.
Procedure review.
Re-performance.
Inquiry.
5-5.e: Inquiry involves asking management and staff for an explanation of what they do to execute a particular control. Inquiry is the least reliable method of testing a control, because people do not necessarily tell the truth.
wrongWhy: Inspection is examination of documents related to control procedures, for example backup to accounting entries. Inspection usually provides better information than some other tests do, so it is not the least reliable testing method.
Procedure review is not one of the four types of tests that are usually used to test the adequacy of internal controls.

Re-performance is manually executing the control, for example recalculating something that a system has automatically calculated or recalculating something that was originally calculated manually. Re-performance is the most reliable method of control testing that can be used to demonstrate operating effectiveness.

5-5.q: Act) The principal impetus for the enactment of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act by the U.S. Congress was to
o: prevent the bribery of foreign officials by U.S. firms seeking to do business overseas.
require mandatory documentation of the evaluation of internal controls by the independent auditors.
discourage unethical behavior by foreigners employed by U.S. firms.
promote the mandates issued by the United Nations with regard to global trade between its member nations.
5-5.e: The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act of 1977 (substantially revised in 1988 and amended in 1998 by the International Anti-Bribery and Fair Competition Act of 1998) was enacted in response to disclosures of questionable payments that had been made by large companies. Investigations by the SEC had revealed that over 400 U.S. companies had made questionable or illegal payments in excess of $300 million to foreign government officials, politicians and political parties. The payments were either illegal political contributions or payments to foreign officials that bordered on bribery.
wrongWhy: The principal impetus for the enactment of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act by the U.S. Congress was not to require mandatory documentation of the evaluation of internal controls by the independent auditors. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The principal impetus for the enactment of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act by the U.S. Congress was not to discourage unethical behavior by foreigners employed by U.S. firms. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The principal impetus for the enactment of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act by the U.S. Congress was not to promote the mandates issued by the United Nations with regard to global trade between its member nations. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-5.q: Act) The fundamental mandate of Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act is the requirement that senior management of public companies must
o: establish and maintain adequate internal controls.
provide real time disclosure of material changes in operational conditions.
maintain a code of ethics for all senior financial officers.
attest to the fair and appropriate presentation of financial statements.
5-5.e: This is the fundamental mandate of Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. Sarbanes-Oxley requires that each annual (10K) or quarterly (10Q) financial report filed or submitted to the SEC in accordance with the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 must include certifications by the company’s principal executive officer or officers and its principal financial officer or officers, as follows: 1. The signing officer has reviewed the report. 2. Based on the signing officer's knowledge, the report does not contain any untrue material statements or omit any material fact that could cause the report to be misleading. 3. Based on the signing officer's knowledge, the financial statements and all the other related information in the report present fairly, in all material respects, the financial condition and results of operations of the company for all the periods presented in the report. 4. The signing officers certify that they (a) are responsible for establishing and maintaining internal controls; (b) have designed the internal controls to ensure that they are made aware of all material information relating to the company and all subsidiaries; have evaluated the effectiveness of the company's internal controls within the previous ninety days; and have presented in the report their conclusions about the effectiveness of their internal controls, based on their evaluation as of the report date. 5. The signing officers have disclosed to the company's auditors and the audit committee of the board of directors (a) all significant deficiencies in the design or operation of the company's internal controls; and (b) any fraud, regardless of its materiality, that involves management or other employees who have a significant role in the company's internal controls. 6. The signing officers have stated in the report whether or not there were any significant changes in internal controls or other factors that could significantly affect internal controls after the date of their evaluation and any corrective actions taken.
wrongWhy: This is not the fundamental mandate of Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. However, the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA) requires corporate management to maintain books, records, and accounts that accurately and fairly reflect transactions and to devise and maintain a system of internal accounting control. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
This is not the fundamental mandate of Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

This is not the fundamental mandate of Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. The SarbanesOxley Act does not require public companies to maintain a code of ethics for all senior financial officers. However, Section 406 of the Act does require every company that is registered with the SEC to disclose whether or not it has adopted a code of ethics for its principal executive officer, principal financial officer, principal accounting officer or controller, or persons performing similar functions, any waivers of the code for those individuals, and any changes made to the code. If the company does not have a code of ethics for those individuals, it must explain why it has not adopted one. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-5.q: A major impact of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act is that registrants subject to the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 are required to
o: Prepare financial statements in accord with international accounting standards.
Keep records that reflect the transactions and dispositions of assets and to maintain a system of internal accounting controls.
Produce full, fair, and accurate periodic reports on foreign commerce and/or foreign political party affiliations.
Provide access to records by authorized agencies of the federal government.
5-5.e: The books and records provision of the accounting provisions of the FCPA requires all issuers of securities that are subject to the SEC to make and keep books, records, and accounts which accurately and fairly in reasonable detail reflect the transactions and dispositions of the assets of the issuer. The Act's internal control provisions require issuers to devise and maintain a system of internal accounting controls sufficient to provide reasonable assurance that: transactions are executed in accordance with management's general or specific authorization; transactions are recorded as necessary to permit preparation of financial statements in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles or any other criteria applicable to such statements, and to maintain accountability for assets; access to assets is permitted only in accordance with management's general or specific authorization; and the recorded accountability for assets is compared with the existing assets at reasonable intervals and appropriate action is taken with respect to any differences.
wrongWhy: The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act does not require companies subject to the Securities Exchange Act toprepare financial statements in accord with international accounting standards. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

TThe Foreign Corrupt Practices Act does not require companies subject to the Securities Exchange Act to produce full, fair, and accurate periodic reports on foreign commerce and/or foreign political party affiliations. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act does not require companies subject to the Securities Exchange Act to provide access to records by authorized agencies of the federal government. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-5.q: Which one of the following best describes an important provision of the U. S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act?
o: The CEO and CFO must certify that they have no knowledge of any corrupt practices occurring in any overseas subsidiaries of U.S. companies.
The internal accounting controls should be examined and if material weaknesses are found, controls must be strengthened.
Auditors cannot provide bookkeeping or other services related to the accounting records or financial statements of the audit client.
Companies must follow the laws of the company's home country as well as the laws of the countries where any foreign subsidiaries are located.
5-5.e: The purpose of the internal control provision of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act is to prevent illegal payments from being made to foreign officials. The fundamental premise of the internal control requirements of the FCPA is that effective internal control acts as a deterrent to such illegal payments. Under the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act, corporate management is required to devise and maintain a system of internal accounting control sufficient to provide reasonable assurances that: transactions are executed in accordance with management's general or specific authorization; transactions are recorded as necessary to permit preparation of financial statements in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles or any other criteria applicable to such statements, and to maintain accountability for assets; access to assets is permitted only in accordance with management's general or specific authorization; and the recorded accountability for assets is compared with the existing assets at reasonable intervals and appropriate action is taken with respect to any differences. While examining the internal accounting controls and strengthening them if material weaknesses are found is not specifically stated as a provision of the FCPA, it is necessary in order to maintain the system of internal accounting control; and maintaining the system of internal accounting control is a provision of the FCPA. Therefore, it is the best answer choice from among those provided.
wrongWhy: This is not a provision of the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Section 201 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act prescribes services that are outside the scope and practice of independent auditors, and thus auditors cannot provide them. Bookkeeping and other services related to the accounting records or financial statements of the audit client are services that are outside the scope and practice of independent auditors. However, prescribing services that are outside the scope and practice of independent auditors is not a provision of the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
This is not a provision of the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-5.q: All of the following statements regarding the top-down or risk-based approach to the audit of internal control over financial reporting are true except that
o: the approach begins at the financial statement level.
the approach focuses on entity-level controls before moving on to significant accounts and disclosures.
it describes the order in which the audit procedures will be performed.
the auditor should focus on accounts and disclosures that present a reasonable possibility of misstatement.
5-5.e: The top-down or risk-based approach to the audit of internal control over financial reporting as set forth in the Auditing Standards of the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB) does not describe the order in which the audit procedures will be performed. PCAOB 2201.21 states the following: “The auditor should use a top-down approach to the audit of internal control over financial reporting to select the controls to test. "A top-down approach begins at the financial statement level and with the auditor's understanding of the overall risks to internal control over financial reporting. "The auditor then focuses on entity-level controls and works down to significant accounts and disclosures and their relevant assertions. "This approach directs the auditor's attention to accounts, disclosures, and assertions that present a reasonable possibility of material misstatement to the financial statements and related disclosures. The auditor then verifies his or her understanding of the risks in the company's processes and selects for testing those controls that sufficiently address the assessed risk of misstatement to each relevant assertion.”
wrongWhy: This statement regarding the top-down or risk-based approach to the audit of internal control over financial reporting is true. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
This statement regarding the top-down or risk-based approach to the audit of internal control over financial reporting is true. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
This statement regarding the top-down or risk-based approach to the audit of internal control over financial reporting is true. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-5.q: A public corporation that must meet the provisions of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act should have a compliance program that includes all of the following steps except
o: documentation of the corporation's existing internal accounting control systems.
an authorized and properly signed agreement that it will abide by the Act.
a cost/benefit analysis of the controls and the risks that are being minimized.
a system of quality checks to evaluate the internal accounting control system.
5-5.e: An authorized and properly signed agreement that a company will abide by the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act is not a necessary step in complying with the Act.
wrongWhy: The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act requires issuers of securities that are regulated by the SEC to devise and maintain a system of internal accounting controls sufficient to provide reasonable assurance that: transactions are executed in accordance with management's general or specific authorization; transactions are recorded as necessary to permit preparation of financial statements in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles or any other criteria applicable to such statements, and to maintain accountability for assets; access to assets is permitted only in accordance with management's general or specific authorization; and the recorded accountability for assets is compared with the existing assets at reasonable intervals and appropriate action is taken with respect to any differences. In order to comply with the internal control provisions of the FCPA and also in order to have an effective internal control system, a company should have documentation of its existing internal accounting control systems. Documentation of the existing internal accounting control systems is important so that internal auditors and external auditors can review them when doing an audit, and so that management and employees can consult them in order to comply with the requirements for implementing the control system.

The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act requires issuers of securities that are regulated by the SEC to devise and maintain a system of internal accounting controls sufficient to provide reasonable assurance that: transactions are executed in accordance with management's general or specific authorization; transactions are recorded as necessary to permit preparation of financial statements in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles or any other criteria applicable to such statements, and to maintain accountability for assets; access to assets is permitted only in accordance with management's general or specific authorization; and the recorded accountability for assets is compared with the existing assets at reasonable intervals and appropriate action is taken with respect to any differences. In order to comply with the internal control provisions of the FCPA and also in order to have an effective internal control system, a company should have a cost/benefit analysis of the controls and the risks that are being minimized. The cost of the operations of the internal controls should be less than the benefit that is received from them. This will lead to some controls not being implemented and the company accepting some amount of risk because the cost of the necessary controls (in time, money, or both) are greater than the benefit they could provide.

The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act requires issuers of securities that are regulated by the SEC to devise and maintain a system of internal accounting controls sufficient to provide reasonable assurance that: transactions are executed in accordance with management's general or specific authorization; transactions are recorded as necessary to permit preparation of financial statements in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles or any other criteria applicable to such statements, and to maintain accountability for assets; access to assets is permitted only in accordance with management's general or specific authorization; and the recorded accountability for assets is compared with the existing assets at reasonable intervals and appropriate action is taken with respect to any differences. In order to comply with the internal control provisions of the FCPA and also in order to have an effective internal control system, a company needs to have a system of quality checks to evaluate the internal accounting control system. Furthermore, according to Section 404 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act, the company's annual report filed with the SEC must state the responsibility of management for establishing and maintaining an adequate control structure and procedures for financial reporting and must contain an assessment by management of the adequacy of the company's internal control over financial reporting. Management is required to document and test its internal control over financial reporting and report on its effectiveness. In order to be able to assess the effectiveness of the internal controls, management must have some way to evaluate them.

5-5.q: The requirement of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act to devise and maintain adequate internal control is assigned in the act to the
o: Company as a whole with no designation of specific persons or positions.
Chief financial officer.
Board of directors.
Director of internal auditing.
5-5.e: The responsibility to devise and maintain adequate internal control is given to the company as whole and not to any individual or position within the company, although individuals are personally liable nonetheless for their own actions. The company must devise and maintain a system of internal accounting controls sufficient to provide reasonaable assurances that, among other requirements, all transactions are in accordance with management's general, or specific, authorization and are recorded properly.
wrongWhy: No particular position or individual is responsible for compliance with the internal control provisions of the FCPA. Please see the correct answer explanaation for more information.
No particular position or individual is responsible for compliance with the internal control provisions of the FCPA. Please see the correct answer explanaation for more information.
No particular position or individual is responsible for compliance with the internal control provisions of the FCPA. Please see the correct answer explanaation for more information.

5-5.q: Sarbanes-Oxley Act) All of the following statements in regard to the requirements for management’s report on internal controls over financial reporting are true except that the report must
o: include a statement of management's responsibility for establishing effective internal controls over financial reporting.
include a statement that the design of the internal controls is the responsibility of the company's audit committee.
assess the effectiveness of the company's internal controls over financial reporting, as of the end of the period.

include a statement that the registered public accounting firm that audited the financial statements has issued an attestation report on the effectiveness of the company's internal control over financial reporting.
5-5.e: This statement in regard to the requirements for management’s report on internal controls over financial reporting is not true, so this is the correct answer. The company's audit committee is not responsible for the design of the company's internal controls. Management is responsible for establishing and maintaining effective internal control over financial reporting.
wrongWhy: This statement in regard to the requirements for management’s report on internal controls over financial reporting in accordance with the Sarbanes-Oxley Act and the subsequent SEC rules is true, so it is an incorrect answer. Each annual report required by the SEC must include: 1. A statement of management's responsibility for establishing and maintaining adequate internal control over financial reporting. 2. A statement identifying the framework used by management to evaluate the effectiveness of its internal control over financial reporting. 3. Management's assessment of the effectiveness of the company's internal control over financial reporting as of the end of the most recent fiscal year. 4. A statement that the registered public accounting firm that audited the financial statements included in the annual report has issued an attestation report on the effectiveness of the company's internal control over financial reporting.

This statement in regard to the requirements for management’s report on internal controls over financial reporting in accordance with the Sarbanes-Oxley Act and the subsequent SEC rules is true, so it is an incorrect answer. Each annual report required by the SEC must include: 1. A statement of management's responsibility for establishing and maintaining adequate internal control over financial reporting. 2. A statement identifying the framework used by management to evaluate the effectiveness of its internal control over financial reporting. 3. Management's assessment of the effectiveness of the company's internal control over financial reporting as of the end of the most recent fiscal year. 4. A statement that the registered public accounting firm that audited the financial statements included in the annual report has issued an attestation report on the effectiveness of the company's internal control over financial reporting.

This statement in regard to the requirements for management’s report on internal controls over financial reporting in accordance with the Sarbanes-Oxley Act and the subsequent SEC rules is true, so it is an incorrect answer. Each annual report required by the SEC must include: 1. A statement of management's responsibility for establishing and maintaining adequate internal control over financial reporting. 2. A statement identifying the framework used by management to evaluate the effectiveness of its internal control over financial reporting. 3. Management's assessment of the effectiveness of the company's internal control over financial reporting as of the end of the most recent fiscal year. 4. A statement that the registered public accounting firm that audited the financial statements included in the annual report has issued an attestation report on the effectiveness of the company's internal control over financial reporting. PCAOB Audit Standard 2201, which applies to independent auditors, also requires the auditor to attest to the effectiveness of the company's internal control over financial reporting.

5-5.q: According to the Sarbanes-Oxley Act, who is responsible for appointing, approving the compensation of, and overseeing the work of the registered public accounting firm?
o: The chief financial officer.
The audit committee.
Management.
The chief executive officer.
5-5.e: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 put more responsibility in the hands of the audit committee of the board of directors. Section 301 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act mandated that the audit committee is to be responsible for appointing, approving the compensation of, and overseeing the work of the public accounting firm. Accordingly, the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 was amended to add the requirement as Rule 10A 3(b)(2).
wrongWhy: The audit committee, not the chief financial officer, is responsible for appointing, approving the compensation of, and overseeing the work of the public accounting firm.
The audit committee, not management, is responsible for appointing, approving the compensation of, and overseeing the work of the public accounting firm.
The audit committee, not the CEO, is responsible for appointing, approving the compensation of, and overseeing the work of the public accounting firm.

5-5.q: Sarbanes-Oxley Act) In accordance with the Sarbanes-Oxley Act, which one of the following certifications is not included in periodic statutory financial reports?
o: The major internal control provisions of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act.
The signing officers have reviewed the report and the report does not contain any material untrue statements or material omissions.
Any significant changes in internal controls or related factors that could have a negative impact are disclosed.
A list of all deficiencies in the internal controls and information on any fraud that involves employees who are involved with internal operations.
5-5.e: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act does not require certification of the major internal control provisions of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act.
wrongWhy: Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act requires that each quarterly or annual report submitted to the SEC include certifications by the company's principal executive officer and its principal financial officer indicating that (1) the officer has reviewed the report, (2) the report does not contain any untrue material misstatement or omission that would cause the report to be misleading, and (3) to the best of the officer's knowledge, the financial statements and all other related information in the report fairly present in all material respects the financial condition and results of operations of the company for all the periods presented in the report.

Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act requires that each quarterly or annual report submitted to the SEC include certifications by the company's principal executive officer and its principal financial officer indicating that, among other things, to the best of the officer's knowledge, the financial statements and all other related information in the report fairly present in all material respects the financial condition and results of operations of the company for all the periods presented in the report. The signing officers must also certify that they have stated in their report whether or not there were any significant changes in internal controls or in any other factors that could have a negative impact on the company's internal controls after the date of their evaluation.

Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act requires that each quarterly or annual report submitted to the SEC include certifications by the company's principal executive officer and its principal financial officer indicating that, among other things, to the best of the officer's knowledge, the financial statements and all other related information in the report fairly present in all material respects the financial condition and results of operations of the company for all the periods presented in the report. The signing officers must also certify that they have disclosed any fraud, regardless of how material it is, that involves management or other employees who have a significant role in the company's internal controls.

5-5.q: Sarbanes-Oxley Act) Title IV, Section 404 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act requires management to assess the adequacy of the company’s internal control over financial reporting and report on it as part of its annual report filed with the SEC. The SEC’s guidance for the management assessment of internal control over financial reporting tells management to focus on
o: the design of the system.
key controls, the controls needed to adequately address the risk of a material misstatement in the financial statements.
using a bottom-up approach to selecting controls for testing.
performing the four types of internal control tests.
5-5.e: According to the SEC’s guidance for management in its assessment of internal control over financial reporting, management should use a top-down approach. Management is to focus on key controls, that is, controls that are needed to adequately address the risk of a material misstatement in its financial statements.
wrongWhy: While the design of the system and of individual controls is important, that should not be the focus of the assessment, according to SEC guidance. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

According to the SEC’s guidance for management in its assessment of internal control over financial reporting, a bottom-up approach is not recommended. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

While the four types of internal control tests may be used in the management assessment of internal control over financial reporting, detail controls should not be the focus of the assessment, according to SEC guidance. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-5.q: Which one of the following statements best describes the internal control requirements of the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act of 1977?
o: It is unlawful to bribe foreign government officials to obtain or retain business.
All major cash payments and receipts must be reported to the U.S. Department of the Treasury.
Management must establish systems to provide assurances that transactions are authorized.
It is unlawful to bribe officers or officials of foreign corporations to obtain or retain business.
5-5.e: This is the statement that best describes the internal control provision of the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act. The FCPA requires corporations covered by its provisions to (a) make and keep books and records that accurately and fairly reflect the transactions of the corporation and (b) devise and maintain an adequate system of internal accounting controls sufficient to provide reasonable assurances that: transactions are executed in accordance with management's general or specific authorization; transactions are recorded as necessary to permit preparation of financial statements in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles or any other criteria applicable to such statements, and to maintain accountability for assets; access to assets is permitted only in accordance with management's general or specific authorization; and the recorded accountability for assets is compared with the existing assets at reasonable intervals and appropriate action is taken with respect to any differences. .
wrongWhy: The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act does make it unlawful to bribe foreign government officials to obtain or retain business. However, that is the anti-bribery provision of the FCPA, not the internal control provision of the FCPA.
This is not a requirement of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act.

This is not a requirement of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act. The anti-bribery provision of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act does make it unlawful to bribe foreign government officials to obtain or retain business, but it does not refer to officers or officials of foreign corporations. Furthermore, the question asks for the statement that best describes the internal control requirements of the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act, not the anti-bribery provision.

5-5.q: Under SOX Section 404, who is responsible for assessing the effectiveness of Internal Control over Financial Reporting (ICFR)?
o: The external auditor only
The internal audit department only
Management — and the external auditor must attest to the assessment (for large accelerated filers)
The audit committee
5-5.e: SOX 404: Management must establish, maintain, and assess the effectiveness of ICFR in the annual report. For large accelerated filers, the External Auditor must also attest to management's assessment. The framework used must be specified — COSO is the standard. This is one of the most costly but important SOX requirements.

5-5.q: SOX Section 302 requires that:
o: External auditors certify the financial statements
The CEO And CFO personally certify the accuracy and completeness of financial reports
The audit committee reviews all transactions
Management hires external auditors directly
5-5.e: SOX 302: The CEO and CFO must Personally Certify each quarterly and annual report — attesting that the statements fairly present the financial condition, that they have disclosed all significant deficiencies in internal controls, and that no fraud was concealed. Criminal penalties apply for false certifications (Section 906).

5-5.q: Under SOX, the audit committee must be composed of:
o: At least one management representative and four independent directors
All Independent directors — no management members; at least one financial expert required
Majority management with one outside director
Any combination the board chooses
5-5.e: SOX requires All audit committee members to be Independent directors (no current or recent ties to the company as employees or consultants). At least One must qualify as a 'financial expert.' The audit committee oversees the external auditor — not management — to prevent management from pressuring auditors.

5-5.q: Which of the following services is Prohibited for external auditors to provide to their audit clients under SOX?
o: Tax compliance services
Internal audit outsourcing to the audit client
Review of interim financial statements
Agreed-upon procedures engagements
5-5.e: SOX Prohibits external auditors from providing Internal Audit Outsourcing to their audit clients — this would be a self-review threat (auditing work they performed). Other prohibited services: bookkeeping, financial IS design/implementation, appraisal/valuation, actuarial, management/HR functions, legal services. Tax services are generally still allowed.

5-5.q: The lead audit partner rotation requirement under SOX requires rotation every:
o: 3 years
10 years
5 Years — mandatory rotation of the lead audit partner
Every year
5-5.e: SOX requires the Lead Audit Partner to rotate every Five Years (not the entire audit firm). This prevents over-familiarity and relationship-based compromises of independence. The cooling-off period after rotation is also 5 years before the same partner can return to the engagement.

5-5.q: SOX (Sarbanes-Oxley Act) applies Primarily to:
o: All US companies regardless of size
Publicly Traded companies registered with the SEC
Only Fortune 500 companies
All companies including non-profits and governmental entities
5-5.e: SOX applies to Publicly Traded companies registered with the SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission). Private companies are Not required to comply, though many adopt SOX principles as best practice. Foreign private issuers listed on US exchanges are also subject to SOX requirements, though some provisions are modified.

5-6.q: For true independence, the Chief Audit Executive (Cae) should report Functionally to:
o: The CEO
The CFO
The Audit Committee of the Board
The Controller
5-6.e: The Cae must report Functionally to the Audit Committee (not management) to maintain independence. If the Cae reports only to the CFO or CEO, management could restrict audit scope or suppress unfavorable findings. Administrative reporting to the CEO for day-to-day matters is acceptable, but the Audit Committee must have ultimate oversight.

5-6.q: The Primary purpose of internal auditing is to:
o: Issue an opinion on financial statements for external users
Detect and prosecute all fraud in the organization
Add Value by evaluating and improving risk management, control, and governance
Prepare the company's tax returns
5-6.e: Per the Iia definition, internal auditing adds Value by providing independent, objective assurance and consulting to help organizations achieve their objectives through evaluation of governance, risk management, and control. It is Not limited to fraud detection or financial statement audit — it covers All business operations.

5-6.q: Which of the following is an Assurance engagement (not a consulting engagement) for internal audit?
o: Advising management on designing a new control process
Training the accounting team on IFRS
Evaluating the Effectiveness of existing internal controls over financial reporting
Recommending improvements to the budgeting process
5-6.e: Assurance engagements = objective assessment of a process/control/system — internal audit independently evaluates effectiveness and reports findings. Consulting engagements = advisory services where management requests help designing or improving systems. Key distinction: assurance = evaluating what exists; consulting = advising on what to build/change.

5-6.q: A risk-based audit plan prioritizes audit resources based on:
o: Equal time allocation to all departments
The CFO's personal preferences
Areas with Highest Risk — where controls may be weakest and impact is greatest
Alphabetical order of business units
5-6.e: Risk-Based Audit Planning focuses resources where they matter most: high-risk areas with potentially weak controls and significant consequences. This maximizes the Value of internal audit. The audit plan is based on the organization's risk assessment and must be flexible to respond to emerging risks. Low-risk areas may be audited less frequently.

5-6.q: Internal audit standards are issued by:
o: PCAOB (Public Company Accounting Oversight Board)
FASB (Financial Accounting Standards Board)
Iia — Institute of Internal Auditors
Aicpa only
5-6.e: The Iia (Institute of Internal Auditors) issues the International Professional Practices Framework (Ippf), which includes the mandatory Standards for the Professional Practice of Internal Auditing. PCAOB sets standards for external auditors of public companies. Aicpa sets standards for external auditors of private companies. FASB sets accounting standards.

5-6.q: The International Professional Practices Framework (Ippf) issued by the Iia includes mandatory guidance consisting of:
o: Optional guidelines and best practices only
The Core Principles, Definition, Code Of Ethics, And Standards — all mandatory for internal auditors
Only the Code of Ethics
Only the Standards for the Professional Practice of Internal Auditing
5-6.e: The Iia's Ippf has two categories: Mandatory guidance (Core Principles, Definition of Internal Auditing, Code of Ethics, and the Standards) which must be followed; and Recommended guidance (Implementation Guidance, Supplemental Guidance) which provides best practices. The mandatory elements define what constitutes professional internal audit practice.

5-6.q: For internal audit to maintain Organizational Independence, the Chief Audit Executive should have:
o: The ability to approve their own audit budget
Unrestricted Access to all records, personnel, and facilities and no operational responsibilities that they would audit
Direct oversight of the accounting department
The authority to hire and fire accounting staff
5-6.e: Organizational Independence requires: (1) Unrestricted Access to all information needed for audits; (2) No Operational Responsibilities in areas being audited (auditing work you control = self-review threat); (3) functional reporting to the Audit Committee (not management). Without independence, internal audit cannot objectively assess controls.

5-6.q: An Operational Audit by the internal audit department evaluates:
o: The accuracy of financial statements
Compliance with tax laws
The Efficiency And Effectiveness Of Operations — whether resources are used optimally to achieve objectives
Only IT system controls
5-6.e: Operational Audit: evaluates the efficiency and effectiveness of operations — are resources (money, people, equipment) being used optimally to achieve organizational objectives? Examples: evaluating procurement processes for cost savings, assessing warehousing efficiency, reviewing the sales process. Much broader than financial audit — focuses on Performance, not just financial accuracy.

5-6.q: An internal Compliance Audit determines whether:
o: Financial statements are fairly stated
Operations are efficient
The Organization IS Adhering to applicable laws, regulations, contracts, and internal policies
Information systems are secure
5-6.e: Compliance Audit: determines adherence to external requirements (laws, regulations, environmental rules, industry standards) and internal policies (code of conduct, operational procedures). Examples: verifying employment law compliance, checking that loan covenants are met, confirming environmental permits are followed. Different from financial audit (FS accuracy) or operational audit (efficiency).

5-6.q: Which of the following Best describes the Three Lines Model in risk management?
o: External audit, internal audit, and the board
First Line: Management owns and manages risks; Second Line: Risk and compliance functions oversee; Third Line: Internal audit provides independent assurance
Operations, finance, and legal departments
Preventive, detective, and corrective controls
5-6.e: Three Lines Model (Iia, updated 2020 — formerly called 'Three Lines of Defense'): (1) First Line — operational management owns risks and implements controls day-to-day; (2) Second Line — risk management and compliance functions oversee first line, provide expertise and monitoring; (3) Third Line — internal audit provides Independent assurance to the board/audit committee on the effectiveness of the first two lines. The 2020 update shifted emphasis from defense to value creation and partnership with the board.

5-6.q: The Internal Audit Charter must include:
o: The detailed audit plan for the coming year
Budget allocations for all audit projects
The Purpose, Authority, And Responsibility of the internal audit function — approved by the audit committee
A list of all past audit findings
5-6.e: The Iia Standards require an Internal Audit Charter that: (1) establishes the internal audit activity's Purpose (why it exists); (2) defines Authority (access rights, ability to report to board); (3) defines Responsibility (what it is accountable for). It must be Approved by the board/audit committee and periodically reviewed. The charter is the foundational document that enables independence.

5-6.q: In the dual reporting structure for internal audit, 'Administrative Reporting' to the CEO means:
o: The CEO controls what internal audit investigates
The CEO can restrict internal audit's access
Day-to-day administrative matters: budget, staffing, compensation — Not audit scope or findings
The CEO approves all internal audit reports before the board sees them
5-6.e: Dual Reporting: Internal audit reports Administratively to the CEO (budget, HR, day-to-day administration) and Functionally to the Audit Committee (audit scope, access, findings, compensation of Cae). This structure maintains independence — the audit committee, not management, has final authority over what internal audit investigates and reports.

5-6.q: A Follow-Up audit by internal audit is performed to:
o: Repeat the same audit procedures from the original engagement
Verify That Management Has Implemented the corrective actions agreed upon after the original audit
Issue a new opinion on the financial statements
Expand the scope of the original audit
5-6.e: Follow-Up audits verify that management has Implemented the corrective actions agreed to in response to original audit findings. Without follow-up, audit findings may never be remediated. Follow-up is a key part of the audit cycle and demonstrates that internal audit drives actual improvement, not just issuing reports. The Iia Standards require internal audit to establish a follow-up process.

5-6.q: Internal audit Adds Value to the organization primarily by:
o: Replacing the external audit and reducing audit fees
Guaranteeing that no fraud will occur in the organization
Providing Independent Assurance And Consulting that improves governance, risk management, and control — helping achieve objectives
Preparing financial statements for management
5-6.e: Per the Iia definition, internal audit Adds Value by providing Independent, Objective assurance and consulting to help the organization achieve its objectives through evaluation and improvement of Governance, Risk Management, and Control processes. It is not just a compliance function — it is a strategic partner that helps the organization improve and manage risk.

5-6.q: An External Quality Assessment of the internal audit function is required by Iia Standards:
o: Every year
Every five years or more frequently — conducted by a qualified independent reviewer from outside the organization
Only when a major fraud is discovered
Voluntarily, with no mandatory frequency
5-6.e: Iia Standards require an External Quality Assessment (Eqa) at least every Five Years by a qualified, independent reviewer (from outside the organization). This 'audit of the auditors' evaluates whether internal audit conforms to Iia Standards and adds value. Organizations use the Eqa conclusion as evidence of internal audit quality to the board and senior management.

5-6.q: When internal audit conducts a Fraud Investigation, the engagement is classified as:
o: An assurance engagement only
A consulting engagement only
A Special Purpose Engagement — involving both assurance elements (gathering evidence) and advisory elements (recommending remediation)
An external audit function
5-6.e: Fraud Investigations are special purpose engagements that combine: (1) Assurance elements — systematically gathering and analyzing evidence to determine what happened, who was involved, and the magnitude; (2) Advisory elements — recommending control improvements to prevent recurrence. The Cae coordinates with legal counsel, HR, and law enforcement as appropriate. Evidence must be handled carefully to preserve chain of custody.

5-6.q: The Scope of internal audit encompasses:
o: Only financial transactions above a materiality threshold
Only compliance with laws and regulations
All Activities That Affect Governance, Risk Management, And Control — operations, finance, compliance, IT, and strategy
Only pre-approved areas in the annual audit plan with no flexibility
5-6.e: Internal audit has a Broad Scope — it can examine Any area of the organization that affects governance, risk management, and control. This includes financial processes, operations, IT systems, compliance, supply chain, strategic initiatives, and culture. The audit plan prioritizes high-risk areas, but the Cae must have authority to investigate any area. Restrictions on scope compromise independence.

5-6.q: Risk-Based Audit Planning means that audit resources are focused on:
o: Equal coverage of all departments on a rotating basis
Areas that management specifically requests be audited
Areas With The Highest Risk — where controls may be weakest and the financial/operational impact of failure is greatest
Areas with the most employees
5-6.e: Risk-Based Audit Planning: internal audit prioritizes the Highest Risk areas — where likelihood of failure is greatest, where controls may be weakest, and where the consequences of failure would be most significant (financial loss, regulatory penalty, reputational damage). This maximizes the value of limited audit resources and ensures audit coverage aligns with actual organizational risk.

5-6.q: The Difference between an internal auditor and an external auditor in terms of independence is that:
o: Internal auditors are more independent than external auditors
External auditors are employees of the company they audit
External Auditors are independent From the company (not employed); internal auditors are independent Within the company (no operational responsibilities in areas they audit)
Both have identical independence requirements
5-6.e: External Auditor independence = independence From the organization — they are completely separate, hired by shareholders/audit committee, and cannot be employees. Internal Auditor independence = organizational independence Within the entity — they are employees but should have no operational responsibilities in areas they audit, report functionally to the audit committee, and maintain objectivity on specific engagements.

5-6.q: According to Iia Standards, internal audit should assess and contribute to improving which three areas?
o: Financial reporting, tax compliance, and audit fees
Revenue recognition, cost accounting, and cash management
Governance, Risk Management, And Control Processes
Operations, marketing, and strategy
5-6.e: The Iia's definition of internal auditing specifically states that internal audit helps organizations achieve objectives by evaluating and improving the effectiveness of: (1) Governance — board and management oversight; (2) Risk Management — identification and management of risks; (3) Control Processes — operations, financial reporting, compliance. These three pillars define internal audit's purpose and scope.

5-7.q: Which type of fraud is the Most Common according to the Acfe (Association of Certified Fraud Examiners)?
o: Financial statement fraud
Corruption
Asset Misappropriation — cash theft, payroll fraud, expense schemes
Tax evasion
5-7.e: Asset Misappropriation is by far the most Common fraud (approximately 89% of cases in Acfe reports) — involving theft or misuse of company assets. Financial statement fraud is the Least common but causes the Most financial damage per case. Corruption falls in between in both frequency and dollar impact.

5-7.q: The Fraud Diamond adds which fourth element to the Fraud Triangle?
o: Detection
Concealment
Capability — the fraudster must have the skills and authority to commit and conceal the fraud
Collusion
5-7.e: The Fraud Diamond adds Capability to Pressure, Opportunity, and Rationalization. Not everyone with the other three elements will commit fraud — they need the skills, intelligence, access, and authority to actually execute and Conceal it. This is why segregation of duties targets capability — even if someone has pressure and rationalization, removing their opportunity/capability stops fraud.

5-7.q: According to Benford's Law, in a naturally occurring data set, which digit should appear as the First Digit most frequently?
o: 1 — appears approximately 30% of the time
5-7.e: Benford'S Law: In naturally occurring financial data, the digit '1' should appear as the first digit approximately 30% of the time, '2' about 18%, and higher digits decreasingly less. Fraudsters often use round numbers (e.g., $500, $9,999) or repeated numbers — these create abnormal first-digit distributions that data analytics can flag.

5-7.q: Skimming differs from Larceny in that skimming involves theft:
o: After cash is recorded in the accounting system
Before cash is recorded — it never enters the books
Of inventory from the warehouse
Of fixed assets from company premises
5-7.e: Skimming = theft of cash Before it is recorded (goes off the books immediately — hardest to detect). Larceny = theft of cash that Has already been recorded (leaves an audit trail). Both are asset misappropriation schemes. Skimming is harder to detect because there is no record of the cash that was taken.

5-7.q: The Most Effective method of fraud Detection according to Acfe research is:
o: External audit
Management review
Whistleblower hotlines / tips from employees
Internal audit
5-7.e: Acfe research consistently shows that Tips (from employees, customers, vendors) through whistleblower hotlines are the Most common fraud detection method — accounting for 40%+ of detected cases. This is why SOX requires audit committees to establish anonymous reporting mechanisms. Internal and external audits are less effective detection methods than many assume.

5-7.q: Financial Statement Fraud is the Least Common but Most Costly type of fraud because:
o: It is too difficult to execute successfully
Only small amounts are usually involved
It typically involves senior management manipulating large accounts — median losses per case are Far Higher than asset misappropriation
Regulators always detect it immediately
5-7.e: Financial statement fraud: Least common (around 9% of cases) but Highest median loss (millions per case) because: (1) perpetrated by Senior Management who control large accounts; (2) schemes involve overstating revenues or understating liabilities — affecting entire financial statements; (3) much harder to detect because those committing it control the controls. Enron and WorldCom are examples.

5-7.q: Corruption as a fraud type includes all of the following Except:
o: Accepting kickbacks from vendors in exchange for selecting them
Creating fictitious vendor invoices to steal company cash
Bribery of government officials to obtain contracts
Bid rigging — arranging for a preferred vendor to win a contract
5-7.e: Corruption involves misuse of one's position for personal gain: bribery (paying to influence), kickbacks (receiving for directing business), conflicts of interest, bid rigging. Creating Fictitious Vendor Invoices is Asset Misappropriation (billing schemes) — theft of company assets through a fraudulent payment, not corruption. The distinction: corruption involves an external party (bribe/kickback); billing schemes are purely internal theft.

5-7.q: Lapping is a common fraud scheme in accounts receivable that involves:
o: Recording sales that were never made
Using Cash From One Customer'S Payment to cover a previously stolen amount from another customer — creating a perpetual cycle of covering shortages
Destroying customer invoices to avoid detection
Overstating the allowance for doubtful accounts
5-7.e: Lapping: the fraudster steals Customer A's payment, then uses Customer B's payment to cover Customer A's account, then uses Customer C's to cover B — creating a perpetual cycle. The Ar balance never reconciles. Detection: compare dates checks are received vs. dates credited to customer accounts — there will be a consistent time delay. Control: person receiving cash should not also apply cash to accounts receivable.

5-7.q: A 'Ghost Employee' payroll scheme involves:
o: Paying real employees twice for the same period
An employee approving their own expense reports
Adding Fictitious Employees to the payroll system and collecting the payments
Manipulating time records to receive overtime not earned
5-7.e: Ghost Employees: fictitious employees added to the payroll by a dishonest HR or payroll employee who then collects the direct deposit or check payments. Controls: HR must independently authorize all hires (separate from payroll); periodic payroll register review by management; direct deposit to employee-owned accounts verified; regular headcount reconciliation between HR records and payroll.

5-7.q: Channel Stuffing as a financial statement fraud scheme involves:
o: Delaying recording of expenses to inflate profit
Recording revenue for goods that customers Have Not Ordered, shipped at period-end to inflate sales, with the understanding they will be returned
Understating cost of goods sold
Recording fictitious vendor payments
5-7.e: Channel Stuffing: sending products to distributors/retailers at period-end Beyond what they ordered or can sell, recording full revenue, knowing the goods will be returned in the next period. It Inflates current period revenue to hit targets. Revenue recognition controls (ASC 606 — performance obligation must be satisfied) should prevent this, but management override can circumvent it.

5-7.q: Expense Reimbursement fraud schemes include all of the following Except:
o: Submitting fictitious receipts for expenses never incurred
Submitting personal expenses as business expenses
Inflating the amount of legitimate expenses
Depositing Customer Checks In A Personal Account before they are recorded
5-7.e: Depositing customer checks in a personal account is Skimming/Larceny (cash theft) — not expense reimbursement fraud.
