Expense reimbursement fraud involves fraudulent or inflated Reimbursement Claims: fictitious receipts (fake hotel/meal receipts), personal expenses disguised as business (spouse's vacation), inflated amounts (claiming $150 for a $50 meal), or duplicate submissions (same receipt twice).

5-7.q: Which behavioral red flag is Most commonly associated with employees committing fraud?
o: Arriving at work early every day
Living A Lifestyle Significantly Beyond What Their Known Salary Would Support
Being very friendly with vendors
Working standard hours and taking all vacation time
5-7.e: An employee living Beyond their means (luxury car, expensive home, lavish vacations incompatible with their salary) is a classic behavioral red flag for fraud. The excess lifestyle often requires a fraudster to continue and escalate the scheme to maintain it. Other behavioral red flags: unusual stress, reluctance to take vacation (covering tracks requires being present), excessive overtime, close personal relationships with vendors.

5-7.q: The Association Of Certified Fraud Examiners (Acfe) definition of Occupational Fraud is:
o: Any crime committed by an employee against a customer
The Use Of One'S Occupation For Personal Enrichment through the deliberate misuse or misapplication of the employer's resources or assets
Fraud committed by executives only
Any regulatory violation by a public company
5-7.e: Acfe defines occupational fraud as: 'the use of one's occupation for personal enrichment through the deliberate misuse or misapplication of the employing organization's resources or assets.' This encompasses asset misappropriation, financial statement fraud, and corruption — all three major categories. 'Occupational' means fraud committed Against the employing organization by insiders.

5-7.q: Data Analytics as a fraud detection method using Benford'S Law would flag which of the following as suspicious?
o: Invoices with first digits distributed normally (1 appearing 30% of the time)
An Unusually High Frequency Of Invoices Starting With '9' — suggesting amounts just below approval thresholds
All invoices above $10,000 regardless of digit
Invoices paid within 30 days
5-7.e: Benford's Law: in natural data, '1' appears as the first digit ~30%, '2' ~18%, etc. Fraudsters who create fictitious invoices often choose round numbers or amounts just Below approval thresholds (e.g., $9,900 instead of $10,000 to avoid the $10,000 approval requirement). This results in an abnormally High frequency of '9' as the first digit — detectable with Benford's Law analysis.

5-7.q: The Capability element of the Fraud Diamond means that:
o: The fraudster must have a financial motive
Weak controls create the chance to commit fraud
The fraudster must have the Intelligence, Position, Authority, and Knowledge to commit and Conceal the fraud
Management must be aware of the fraud
5-7.e: Capability (Fraud Diamond's 4th element): the fraudster must have: (1) a Position of sufficient authority; (2) knowledge of the specific vulnerabilities; (3) the intelligence to execute and Conceal the scheme; (4) Ego to believe they won't be caught. This explains why not everyone who faces pressure and has opportunity actually commits fraud — they may lack the capability to execute and cover it.

5-7.q: A company implements an Anti-Fraud Training Program for all employees. The Most Direct impact of this program is on which fraud triangle element?
o: Pressure — reducing financial need
Opportunity — strengthening controls
Rationalization — building ethical culture that makes fraud seem unacceptable
Capability — reducing the skill to commit fraud
5-7.e: Anti-fraud training primarily attacks Rationalization: it communicates that fraud IS theft (not 'borrowing'), that the company takes it seriously, that people Do get caught, and that the consequences are severe. This makes it harder for potential fraudsters to justify their actions mentally. Training also helps employees recognize and report fraud, indirectly reducing opportunity.

5-7.q: A Kiting scheme involves:
o: Stealing physical inventory from the warehouse
Taking Advantage Of Float Time — writing checks between bank accounts to temporarily show inflated cash balances
Falsifying employee hours for overtime pay
Diverting customer payments to a personal account
5-7.e: Kiting exploits the Float Time between when a check is written and when it clears. Example: write a check from Bank A to Bank B on Dec 31 (to inflate Bank B balance for year-end) while the check hasn't cleared Bank A yet — both banks show the money simultaneously. Detection: prepare a bank transfer schedule comparing dates recorded vs. dates cleared across accounts.

5-7.q: Which fraud prevention control is Most effective for addressing the Opportunity element of the Fraud Triangle?
o: Anti-fraud training programs
Strong ethical culture
Performance pressure management
Strong Internal Controls And Segregation Of Duties — removing the physical opportunity to commit fraud
5-7.e: Opportunity is primarily reduced by Strong Internal Controls: segregation of duties (no single person controls an entire transaction), physical controls (locked assets), authorization requirements, independent reconciliations, and monitoring. These make it difficult or impossible to commit and conceal fraud. Training reduces rationalization. Ethical culture reduces pressure and rationalization. Only strong controls directly eliminate opportunity.

5-7.q: According to Acfe research, Tips are the #1 fraud detection method. This finding Most supports the importance of:
o: Increasing external audit procedures
Expanding the internal audit department
Robust Whistleblower Programs — anonymous reporting hotlines and channels where employees can report suspicious activity
Hiring more forensic accountants
5-7.e: Acfe's Report to the Nations consistently shows that Tips (mainly from employees, customers, and vendors) account for 40%+ of fraud detections — far more than internal audit, external audit, or management review. This highlights the critical importance of: anonymous whistleblower hotlines, anti-retaliation policies, creating a culture where employees feel safe reporting, and publicizing how tips are handled.

5-7.q: Surprise Audits are highly effective as a fraud Deterrent because:
o: They are less expensive than planned audits
They always catch fraudsters in the act
The Unpredictability Deters Fraud — employees know an unannounced count could happen at any time, making ongoing fraud riskier
They are required by SOX for all public companies
5-7.e: Surprise Audits deter fraud through Unpredictability: if a cashier knows their drawer could be counted unannounced at any time, maintaining a shortage is very risky. The deterrent effect often exceeds the detection value. This is why surprise procedures (unannounced inventory counts, random expense report audits, surprise vendor confirmations) are valuable even if they catch little directly.

5-8.q: Which of the following is not a basic principle of sound corporate governance?
o: A majority of directors should be independent.
There is effective interaction between board, management, the external auditor and the internal auditor.
The roles of Board Chair and Chief Executive Officer (CEO) should be separated.
Only the interests of the shareholders are considered.
5-8.e: Sound corporate governance also recognizes that other stakeholders, such as creditors, vendors, and many others, also have an interest in the operations of a business.
wrongWhy: This is a true statement about sound corporate governance.
This is a true statement about sound corporate governance.
This is a true statement about sound corporate governance.

5-8.q: Good governance states that boards of directors should consist of independent, nonexecutive directors. Some of the ways to ensure independence would be all of the following except
o: service on the board should be for a specific time period.
a director's reappointment to the board should be automatic.
a director should not serve also as a director of a competing company.
a director should not have any financial, business or other connection with the company apart from some compensation fee for service on the board of directors.
5-8.e: This is not true. Generally, board members should stand for re-election every three years and members should not serve more than three consecutive terms.
wrongWhy: This is true. Generally, board members should not serve more than three consecutive 3year terms.

This is true. To maintain independence, a director of a corporation should not also serve as a director of a competitor. The director's loyalties would be divided and the director's duties to the two companies would conflict with each other.

This is true. A director should not enter into any transaction with the company on which he or she serves as a director, such as buying from or selling to the company. To do so would create a conflict of interest for the director.

5-8.q: According to 21st Century Governance Principles for U.S. Public Companies, good governance requires effective interaction among which five entities?
o: The board of directors, the shareholders, the CEO, the CFO, and the Vice President of Marketing.
The corporation, the shareholders, the external auditor, the creditors, and the employees.
The board of directors, the compensation committee, the audit committee, the CEO, and the CFO.
The board of directors, management, the external auditor, the internal auditor, and legal counsel.
5-8.e: The five entities that need to have good interaction in order to have good governance are the board of directors, executive management, the external auditors, the internal auditors, and legal counsel. When these five groups work well and productively with each other, the governance processes are strengthened.
wrongWhy: Effective interaction among the board of directors, the shareholders, the CEO, the CFO, and the Vice President of Marketing is not required for good governance.
Effective interaction among the corporation, the shareholders, the external auditor, the creditors, and the employees is not required for good governance.
Effective interaction among the board of directors, the compensation committee, the audit committee, the CEO, and the CFO is not required for good governance.

5-8.q: Which of the following statements regarding corporate governance is not correct?
o: The dilution of shareholders’ wealth resulting from employee stock options or employee stock bonuses is an accounting issue rather than a corporate governance issue.
The compensation scheme for management is part of the corporate control mechanisms.
Corporate control mechanisms include internal and external mechanisms.
The internal auditor of a company has more responsibility than the board for the company’s corporate governance.
5-8.e: The board is ultimately responsible for the company’s corporate governance, not the internal auditors.
wrongWhy: The dilution of shareholder’s wealth resulting from employee stock options or employee stock bonuses is an accounting issue rather than a corporate governance issue.
Management’s compensation scheme is part of corporate control mechanisms.
Corporate control mechanisms do include internal and external mechanisms.

5-8.q: Corporate governance is best described as
o: the process of implementing strategic plans.
the system of planning and allocating resources.
a system that maximizes shareholder wealth.
the means by which businesses are directed and controlled.
5-8.e: Corporate governance includes all the means by which businesses are directed and controlled, including the rules, regulations, processes, customs, policies, procedures, institutions, and laws that affect the way the business is administered. It is a system by which an organization makes and implements decisions in pursuit of its objectives. Corporate governance spells out the rules and procedures to be followed in making decisions for the corporation for the purpose of meeting its various stakeholders’ objectives. A corporation’s stakeholders include all those who are affected by its actions: its board of directors, its shareholders, its management, its employees, its customers, its suppliers, its creditors, the community, and the government.
wrongWhy: Corporate governance is not the process of implementing strategic plans. The process of implementing strategic plans involves developing tactical plans, which are designed to implement specific parts of the strategic plan. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Corporate governance is not the system of planning and allocating resources. Planning and allocation of resources take place during the strategic planning and budgeting processes. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
Corporate governance is not a system that maximizes shareholder wealth. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-8.q: Which of the following is not an objective of the governance process?
o: Effectively coordinating the activities of and communicating information among the board, external and internal auditors, and management.
Ensuring effective organizational performance management and accountability.
Promoting the effective consolidation of financial reports.
Promoting appropriate ethics and values within the organization.
5-8.e: Promoting the effective consolidation of financial reports is not an objective of the governance process.
wrongWhy: An objective of the governance process is to effectively coordinate the activities of and communicate information among the board, external and internal auditors, and management.
An objective of the governance process is to ensure effective organizational performance management and accountability.
An objective of the governance process is to promote appropriate ethics and values within the organization.

5-8.q: Which of the following would be of greatest concern to an auditor reviewing a policy regarding the sale of a company’s used PCs to outside parties?
o: Whether deleted files on the hard disk drive have been completely erased.
Whether the computer has viruses.
Whether there is terminal emulation software on the computer.
Whether all software on the computer is properly licensed.
5-8.e: While most delete programs erase file pointers, they do not remove the underlying data. The company must use special utilities that fully erase the data. This is important because of the potential for confidential data on the PCs.
wrongWhy: This could create a liability for the company if a virus destroyed the purchasing party’s data or programs. However, the purchasing party should use anti-virus software to detect and eliminate any viruses. This concern, while important, is not as serious as the correct response.
Terminal emulation software is widely available.

The purchasing party has a responsibility to ensure that all their software is properly licensed. If the company represented that all the software was properly licensed, this could create a liability. However, this liability is not as serious as the implication from the correct response.

5-8.q: A principle of good corporate governance is the independence of its directors. Of the following, which director(s) would not be considered independent? I. The director is the brother-in-law of the CEO. II. The director's brother is the CEO of the company's major supplier. III. The director retired as CEO from the company six months ago. IV. The director owns a thousand shares of a mutual fund that has an investment in the company.
o: II and III only.
I and II only.
I, II, and III only.
None of the directors would be considered independent.
5-8.e: An independent director is one who has no professional or personal ties (either current or former) to the company or its management. The directors in situations I, II and III would not be considered independent because of their current personal or former professional ties with the company. However, the director who owns shares in a mutual fund that has an investment in the company would be independent. Mutual funds invest in many different types of companies. The fact that the director has a mutual fund that has investment in the company would not impair the director's independence because the director would not control the specific investments of the mutual fund.
wrongWhy: An independent director is one who has no professional or personal ties (either current or former) to the company or its management. In both II and III, the director has a personal relationship with someone connected to the company and is therefore not independent. However, these are not the only directors who are not independent.

An independent director is one who has no professional or personal ties (either current or former) to the company or its management. In both I and II, the director has a personal relationship with someone connected to the company and is therefore not independent. However, these are not the only directors who are not independent.
The director in one of the situations would be considered independent.

5-8.q: The most common motivation for management fraud is the existence of:
o: Job dissatisfaction.
Vices, such as a gambling habit.
Financial pressures on the organization.
The challenge of committing the perfect crime.
5-8.e: Management fraud benefits organizations rather than individuals, so the existence of financial pressure is the most common motivation. Management perpetrators attempt to make their financial statements appear more attractive because of the financial pressures of restrictive loan covenants, a poor cash position, loss of significant customers, etc.
wrongWhy: See the correct answer for an explanation.
See the correct answer for an explanation.
See the correct answer for an explanation.

5-8.q: All of the following are typically responsibilities of the board of directors except
o: ensuring that the corporation is managed in accordance with applicable laws.
hiring and evaluating the performance of the CEO.
attesting to the effectiveness of the controls over financial reporting.
setting corporate strategy, overall direction, and vision.
5-8.e: Attesting to the effectiveness of the controls over financial reporting is not a responsibility of the board of directors. According to PCAOB Audit Standard 2201 (formerly Audit Standard No. 5), the independent auditor is responsible for attesting to the effectiveness of the company's internal control over financial reporting.
wrongWhy: Ensuring that the corporation is managed in accordance with applicable laws is typically a responsibility of the board of directors.
Hiring and evaluating the performance of the CEO are typically responsibilities of the board of directors.
Setting corporate strategy, overall direction, and vision for a corporation are typically responsibilities of the board of directors.

5-8.q: The responsibility for selecting, nominating, and supervising the external auditor of a publicly-held corporation lies with
o: The chief audit executive.
the audit committee.
the chief executive officer.
senior management.
5-8.e: The audit committee of the board of directors is responsible for selecting and nominating the external auditor, approving audit fees, supervising the external auditor, overseeing auditor qualifications and independence, discussing with the auditors matters required under generally accepted auditing standards, and reviewing the audit scope, plan, and results.
wrongWhy: The responsibility for selecting, nominating, and supervising the external auditor of a publicly-held corporation does not lie with the chief audit executive. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The responsibility for selecting, nominating, and supervising the external auditor of a publicly-held corporation does not lie with the chief executive officer. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The responsibility for selecting, nominating, and supervising the external auditor of a publicly-held corporation does not lie with senior management. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-8.q: Corporations in the United States are chartered by
o: The Securities and Exchange Commission.
State governments.
The board of directors of the respective corporations.
The federal government.
5-8.e: In the United States corporations are chartered by state governments. A corporation is incorporated in a certain state and then must follow the corporate laws of that state.
wrongWhy: In the United States, the Securities and Exchange Commission does not charter corporations.
In the United States, the board of directors of the respective corporation does not charter corporations.
In the United States, the federal government does not charter corporations.

5-8.q: Which of the following is not an appropriate member of an audit committee?
o: An academic specializing in business administration.
A retired executive of a firm that had been associated with the organization.
The organization's vice president of operations.
The vice president of the local bank used by the organization.
5-8.e: Requirements for audit committee members are: 1. They must be independent. That is, they must not be employed by the company in any capacity other than as a member of the audit committee, the board of directors, or any other board committee. 2. One member of the audit committee must have accounting or financial management expertise. 3. All members of the audit committee must be financially literate. The organization's vice president of operations is a member of management and thus would not be independent.
wrongWhy: Requirements for audit committee members are: 1. They must be independent. That is, they must not be employed by the company in any capacity other than as a member of the audit committee, the board of directors, or any other board committee. 2. One member of the audit committee must have accounting or financial management expertise. 3. All members of the audit committee must be financially literate. An academic specializing in business administration would be an independent director and with knowledge of business administration would be financially literate. Thus, this person would be an appropriate member of the audit committee.

Requirements for audit committee members are: 1. They must be independent. That is, they must not be employed by the company in any capacity other than as a member of the audit committee, the board of directors, or any other board committee. 2. One member of the audit committee must have accounting or financial management expertise. 3. All members of the audit committee must be financially literate. A retired executive of a firm that had been associated with the organization would be an independent director and as an executive, would be financially literate. Thus, this person would be an appropriate member of the audit committee.

Requirements for audit committee members are: 1. They must be independent. That is, they must not be employed by the company in any capacity other than as a member of the audit committee, the board of directors, or any other board committee. 2. One member of the audit committee must have accounting or financial management expertise. 3. All members of the audit committee must be financially literate. The vice president of the local bank would be an independent director and, as the vice president of a bank, would be financially literate. Thus, this person would be an appropriate member of the audit committee.

5-8.q: Within the organizational structure of a corporation listed on the New York Stock Exchange, the review of the external auditor's audit plan and the results of the audit would most likely be the responsibility of the corporation's
o: Vice president of finance.
Audit committee of the board of directors.
President.
Controller.
5-8.e: One of the roles of the audit committee is to oversee the external audit. This would include the review of the external auditor's plan and results of the audit. The audit committee is to be composed of members of the board of directors who are independent, in other words, not employed by the company in any capacity.
wrongWhy: The review of the external auditor's plan and results of the audit would not be the responsibility of the vice president of finance of the company.
The review of the external auditor's plan and results of the audit would not be the responsibility of the president of the company.
The review of the external auditor's plan and results of the audit would not be the responsibility of the controller.

5-8.q: The audit committee strengthens the control processes of an organization by
o: Following up on recommendations made by the chief audit executive.
Approving internal audit activity policies.
Assigning the internal audit activity responsibility for interaction with governmental agencies.
Using the chief audit executive as a major resource in selecting the external auditors.
5-8.e: The audit committee should provide support to the internal auditors. One way to do this is to follow up on recommendations made by the chief audit executive.
wrongWhy: Approving internal audit activity policies does not strengthen the control process.
Assigning the internal audit activity responsibility for interaction with governmental agencies does not strengthen the control process.

According the Sarbanes-Oxley, the audit committee is to be directly responsible for the appointment, compensation, and oversight of the registered public accounting firm employed to perform the audit.

5-8.q: Corporate directors, management, external auditors, and internal auditors all play important roles in creating a proper control environment. With respect to the control environment, top management is primarily responsible for
o: Implementing and monitoring controls designed by the board of directors.
Ensuring that external and internal auditors adequately monitor the control environment.
Establishing a proper environment and specifying an overall internal control structure.
Reviewing the reliability and integrity of financial information and the means used to collect and report such information.
5-8.e: It is management's responsibility to establish the proper control environment and to design an overall internal control structure.
wrongWhy: Although the board of directors has oversight responsibility, it is not the responsibility of the board of directors to design the controls.

Management's responsibility is not to ensure that external and internal auditors adequately monitor the control environment, because monitoring the control environment is not a responsibility of auditors. Management's responsibility is to monitor the control environment itself.

Whereas management is responsible for establishing the proper control environment and designing an overall internal control structure, it is the responsibility of internal auditing to review the reliability and integrity of financial information and the means used to collect and report such information.

5-8.q: Responsibilities of directors on a corporation's board of directors include all of the following except to
o: ensure the corporation is in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations.
make astute operating decisions.
select and evaluate senior management.
participate in setting corporate strategy.
5-8.e: Making operating decisions is not a responsibility of the board of directors.
wrongWhy: This is a responsibility of the board of directors.
This is a responsibility of the board of directors.
This is a responsibility of the board of directors.

5-8.q: Which of the following features of a large manufacturer's organizational structure is a control weakness?
o: The information systems department is headed by a vice president who reports directly to the president.
The audit committee of the board consists of the chief executive officer, the chief financial officer, and a major shareholder.
The controller and treasurer report to the chief financial officer.
The chief financial officer is a vice president who reports to the chief executive officer.
5-8.e: According to Sarbanes-Oxley, the audit committee must consist of directors who are independent of management. Thus, the chief executive officer and the chief financial officer may not be included.
wrongWhy: It is appropriate for the vice president in charge of information systems to report directly to the president.
It is appropriate for the controller and the treasurer to report to the chief financial officer.
It is appropriate for the chief financial officer to report to the chief executive officer.

5-8.q: The Agency Problem in corporate governance arises from the conflict between:
o: External auditors and internal auditors
Management (agents) and Shareholders (principals) who have differing interests
The board and external regulators
Debt holders and equity holders
5-8.e: The Agency Problem: shareholders (principals) hire management (agents) to run the company, but managers may act in their Own interest (higher salaries, empire-building, job security) rather than maximizing shareholder value. Corporate governance mechanisms (board oversight, incentive compensation, disclosure requirements) are designed to align agent and principal interests.

5-8.q: A Clawback provision in executive compensation allows:
o: Executives to earn additional bonuses for exceeding targets
The Company To Recover compensation previously paid if it was based on restated financial results
Shareholders to increase executive pay
Executives to defer compensation to reduce taxes
5-8.e: Clawback provisions require executives to Return previously paid compensation if the financial results on which the pay was based are later Restated downward. This discourages short-term earnings manipulation — executives cannot keep bonuses earned by inflating earnings. SOX introduced clawbacks for CEO/CFO; Dodd-Frank expanded them broadly.

5-9.q: According to the Ima Standards of Ethical Conduct, the Competence standard requires management accountants to:
o: Keep all information confidential
Avoid all conflicts of interest
Maintain professional knowledge and perform duties per applicable laws and technical standards
Disclose all material information to external parties
5-9.e: Competence: Maintain professional knowledge and skills at the level needed to ensure competent professional service; perform per applicable laws, regulations, and technical standards; provide accurate information. This requires ongoing continuing education (Cpe) and staying current with accounting standards.

5-9.q: If a management accountant faces an ethical conflict with their immediate supervisor, the Ima recommends First:
o: Resigning from the position
Contacting the external auditor
Discussing the issue with the immediate supervisor (unless they are involved) before escalating
Going directly to the board of directors
5-9.e: Ima Conflict Resolution: Step 1 = discuss with immediate supervisor First (unless supervisor is the problem). Step 2 = escalate to next management level or CFO. Step 3 = contact Ima Ethics Hotline anonymously. Step 4 = consult attorney. Last Resort = resign. Never prepare or sign misleading documents — even under pressure from supervisors.

5-9.q: The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (Fcpa) prohibits:
o: Bribing domestic government officials
US companies from Bribing Foreign Government Officials to obtain or retain business
All forms of corporate entertainment
Paying sales commissions to foreign agents
5-9.e: Fcpa: prohibits US companies (and their agents, subsidiaries, and individuals) from paying Bribes to Foreign Government Officials to obtain, retain, or direct business. It also requires accurate books/records and adequate internal controls (accounting provisions). Facilitation payments to non-officials for routine government actions were historically allowed but are increasingly restricted.

5-9.q: The Ima Confidentiality standard requires a management accountant to:
o: Share all information with shareholders upon request
Keep information confidential except when authorized or legally required to disclose
Share information with the external auditor at any time
Disclose all information to the audit committee
5-9.e: Confidentiality: Keep information confidential and do Not disclose it without proper authorization Unless legally obligated to disclose. This includes not using confidential information for personal advantage. Exceptions: disclosure authorized by management, required by law/regulation, or to report a legal violation. This is different from Integrity which relates to conflicts of interest.

5-9.q: A compliance program's Whistleblower Hotline is designed to:
o: Allow management to monitor employee communications
Provide employees an anonymous channel to report suspected ethical violations or fraud
Replace the internal audit function
Issue public disclosures to investors
5-9.e: Whistleblower Hotlines provide a Confidential and often Anonymous channel for employees, vendors, or customers to report suspected fraud, ethics violations, or control failures. Per Acfe research, tips are the #1 fraud detection method. SOX requires public company audit committees to establish these procedures. Effective hotlines significantly reduce fraud losses.

5-9.q: The Ima Credibility standard requires management accountants to:
o: Maximize the company's reported profits
Report only information favorable to management
Disclose All Relevant Information that could reasonably influence an intended user's understanding — including delays and deficiencies
Keep all financial information confidential
5-9.e: Ima Credibility: (1) communicate information fairly and objectively; (2) Disclose All Relevant Information that could reasonably influence intended users' decisions; (3) Disclose Delays or deficiencies in information, timeliness, processing, or internal controls when relevant. This means not hiding bad news — even if management is displeased, users must receive complete, honest information.

5-9.q: The Ima Integrity standard requires management accountants to Avoid:
o: Seeking continuing professional education
Being objective in providing analysis
Conflicts Of Interest — relationships or financial interests that could compromise their independence
Communicating unfavorable information
5-9.e: Ima Integrity: avoid actual or apparent Conflicts Of Interest; disclose all relationships that might appear to impair objective judgment; refuse gifts, favors, or hospitality that could influence decisions; communicate both favorable And unfavorable information honestly; refrain from activities that would discredit the profession. Integrity = acting honestly even when it's difficult.

5-9.q: Under the Ima Confidentiality standard, a management accountant Should disclose confidential information when:
o: Their supervisor requests it
A colleague asks for information for a work project
Legally Obligated To Disclose — court order, regulatory requirement, or when reporting suspected illegal activity to appropriate authorities
The information would benefit shareholders
5-9.e: Ima Confidentiality: keep information confidential and do not use it for personal advantage Except when: (1) Authorized by management to disclose; (2) Legally Required to disclose (court subpoena, regulatory requirement); (3) reporting to authorities when there is a Legal Violation. This is why auditors can report fraud to regulators even without management consent — legal obligation overrides confidentiality.

5-9.q: The Fcpa's Accounting Provisions apply to:
o: Only companies actively engaged in bribery
Only private US companies
All US Public Companies — requiring accurate books/records and adequate internal controls, even if they have no foreign operations
Only foreign subsidiaries of US companies
5-9.e: Fcpa Accounting Provisions (Section 13(b)): apply to All SEC-registered companies — even those with no foreign operations. They require: (1) Accurate Books And Records — transactions recorded as they actually are, not disguised; (2) Adequate Internal Controls — sufficient to ensure financial statement accuracy. Many Fcpa enforcement actions focus on these accounting provisions, not just the anti-bribery provisions.

5-9.q: When should a management accountant Resign from their position due to an ethical conflict?
o: Whenever they disagree with a management decision
As soon as any ethical issue arises
Only As A Last Resort — after exhausting all internal escalation channels and when the issue cannot be resolved without compromising professional ethics
When the Ima Ethics Hotline advises them to
5-9.e: Resignation is the Last Resort in the Ima conflict resolution process: (1) discuss with supervisor; (2) escalate to higher management/CFO; (3) contact Ima Ethics Hotline; (4) consult attorney. If All channels fail and the only options are to comply with an unethical directive or resign, the accountant must Resign rather than prepare misleading documents. Resignation is not the first response — but it may be the final necessary one.

5-9.q: A Code Of Conduct in a compliance program Primarily serves to:
o: Replace all other internal controls
Communicate The Company'S Values, Ethical Standards, And Expected Behaviors to all employees — setting the behavioral baseline
Fulfill a legal requirement with no practical effect
Apply only to executives and managers
5-9.e: Code Of Conduct: a written document that articulates the company's Values, ethical principles, and Expected Behaviors in specific situations (conflicts of interest, gifts, confidentiality, workplace conduct). It communicates to All employees what is expected, creates a common ethical baseline, and supports a culture of integrity. It is the cornerstone of a compliance program but must be reinforced by training, enforcement, and leadership example.

5-9.q: The Chief Compliance Officer (Cco) or Ethics Officer in an organization is responsible for:
o: Preparing the annual financial statements
Conducting external audits
Designing, Implementing, And Overseeing the compliance and ethics program — including training, monitoring, and enforcement
Approving all capital expenditure requests
5-9.e: The Cco/Ethics Officer: (1) Designs and implements the compliance program (code of conduct, training, policies); (2) Monitors for compliance violations; (3) manages the whistleblower hotline; (4) investigates reported violations; (5) enforces consequences; (6) reports to the board/audit committee on compliance matters. Independence from operational management is important for credibility.

5-9.q: Under Fcpa, 'Facilitation Payments' (small payments to foreign officials for routine government services) were historically:
o: Completely prohibited from the start
Treated identically to major bribes
Historically permitted as an Exception to the Fcpa's anti-bribery provisions — but they are prohibited under the Uk Bribery Act and increasingly restricted
Required to be disclosed to the SEC
5-9.e: Fcpa Facilitation Payments Exception: historically, the Fcpa included an exception for small 'grease payments' to foreign officials for Routine, Non-Discretionary government services (getting utilities connected, processing visas, clearing customs). However: the Uk Bribery Act (2010) has No such exception, and US enforcement has narrowed the exception significantly. Most multinationals now prohibit All facilitation payments to avoid Uk Bribery Act exposure.

5-9.q: The Ima Statement of Ethical Professional Practice specifically states that management accountants should Never:
o: Seek guidance from the Ima Ethics Hotline
Report unfavorable financial results to management
Prepare Or Sign Documents Known To Contain False Or Misleading Information — even under pressure from supervisors
Participate in continuing professional education
5-9.e: The Ima Statement of Ethical Professional Practice explicitly prohibits preparing or signing documents Known to be false or misleading — this is an Absolute prohibition, not a guideline. Even if a supervisor directs it, the management accountant must refuse. The appropriate response is to escalate (not comply), and ultimately resign if necessary. Signing a false document is fraud — regardless of who directed it.

5-9.q: Anti-corruption programs must include which elements to be considered effective under the Doj/SEC guidelines?
o: Only a written anti-corruption policy
Risk Assessment + Policies/Procedures + Training + Testing And Monitoring + Investigation + Remediation — a comprehensive program
Just training employees on Fcpa
Annual certification by the CEO only
5-9.e: Effective Compliance Programs (per Doj/SEC guidance) require: (1) tone from the top; (2) risk-based Policies; (3) Training tailored to roles; (4) Due Diligence on third parties; (5) Controls over books/records; (6) Monitoring and testing; (7) Investigation of violations; (8) Remediation and discipline; (9) continuous improvement. A paper program without real implementation is insufficient — prosecutors evaluate effectiveness.

5-9.q: The Ima Competence standard most directly requires management accountants to:
o: Disclose all relevant information to users
Avoid conflicts of interest
Provide accurate and honest information
Maintain Professional Knowledge And Skills through continuing education and perform duties per applicable standards
5-9.e: Ima Competence: (1) maintain professional Knowledge and skills at the level needed for competent service; (2) perform duties per applicable Laws, Regulations, and technical Standards; (3) prepare complete, accurate, and timely reports/recommendations; (4) stay current with accounting and financial management developments through Cpe. This obligation to maintain competence is why the CMA certification requires continuing education.

5-9.q: A management accountant discovers that their company is systematically over-billing government clients. Under the Ima Code, they should:
o: Ignore it — it is not their responsibility
Document it and wait for the external auditor to find it
Escalate Through Internal Channels First, then consider reporting to the appropriate government authorities if internal resolution fails
Immediately contact the news media
5-9.e: When discovering potential Legal Violations, the Ima process: (1) Escalate Internally — supervisor, CFO, legal counsel, audit committee; (2) if internal channels fail or the violation continues, Reporting To Appropriate Authorities may be required (legal obligation overrides confidentiality). Intentional over-billing of government clients may violate the False Claims Act with whistleblower protections and rewards for reporters.

5-9.q: The purpose of Ethics Training in an organization is Primarily to:
o: Substitute for internal controls
Ensure all employees have law degrees
Build Awareness of ethical issues, reinforce the code of conduct, provide guidance on handling dilemmas, and create a culture where ethical behavior is the norm
Eliminate the need for a whistleblower hotline
5-9.e: Ethics training: (1) Build Awareness of what constitutes ethical violations and how to recognize them; (2) Reinforce the code of conduct and company values; (3) provide Practical Guidance on handling ethical dilemmas; (4) demonstrate management's Commitment to ethics (deterring rationalization). Training alone doesn't create an ethical culture — it must be reinforced by leadership behavior, enforcement, and consequences.

5-9.q: A management accountant receives a gift from a vendor worth $500 after helping select that vendor for a major contract. Under Ima Integrity standards, this situation:
o: Is acceptable as long as the vendor is the best choice
Requires disclosure to the Ima only
Represents A Conflict Of Interest — the gift must be Refused or disclosed, and the relationship to the vendor selection must be reported to management
Is standard business practice and requires no action
5-9.e: Ima Integrity prohibits accepting gifts that could Appear to influence professional judgment. A $500 gift after a vendor selection decision creates a clear Conflict Of Interest — even if the selection was actually objective, it appears compromised. The management accountant should: (1) Refuse the gift or return it; (2) Disclose the situation to management; (3) consider whether they should be recused from future decisions involving this vendor.

5-9.q: The Dodd-Frank Act provides financial Rewards to SEC whistleblowers who report securities violations. The primary Purpose of these rewards is to:
o: Encourage employees to bypass their employer and go directly to regulators
Incentivize Reporting of securities violations — particularly when internal reporting channels have failed or management is involved in the fraud
Punish companies by facilitating regulatory fines
Replace the need for internal compliance programs
5-9.e: Dodd-Frank SEC Whistleblower Program: awards 10-30% of sanctions over $1M to individuals who provide original information about securities violations. Purpose: Incentivize reporting of securities fraud — particularly when the perpetrator IS management (who controls internal channels). It provides financial protection (anti-retaliation) and reward to those who take personal risks to expose fraud. Companies responded by strengthening internal compliance programs to capture issues before they reach the SEC.

5-10.q: A Material Weakness in internal controls is defined as:
o: Any minor control deficiency identified by internal audit
A significant deficiency that is less severe than a material weakness
A significant deficiency (or combination) that creates a Reasonable Possibility of material misstatement in the financial statements
A deficiency only reported to management
5-10.e: Material Weakness: A deficiency (or combination of deficiencies) in ICFR such that there is a Reasonable Possibility that a material misstatement will not be prevented or detected on a timely basis. It must be disclosed Publicly in the annual report under SOX 404. The external auditor issues an adverse opinion on ICFR if a material weakness exists.

5-10.q: A Significant Deficiency in internal controls is:
o: Less important than a control deficiency — requires no action
More severe than a material weakness
Less severe than a material weakness but Important Enough to warrant attention from those charged with governance (audit committee)
Required to be disclosed publicly in the annual report
5-10.e: Significant Deficiency: More serious than a plain control deficiency but Less severe than a material weakness. It must be communicated to the Audit Committee and External Auditor in writing. Unlike a material weakness, it does Not need to be disclosed publicly in the annual report, but management and the board must take it seriously and remediate it.

5-10.q: Ongoing monitoring activities differ from Separate evaluations because ongoing monitoring:
o: Is performed only by external auditors
Consists of Periodic independent reviews, like internal audits
Is Built Into routine operations — supervisory review, system alerts, exception reports — providing continuous feedback
Is only triggered when a fraud is suspected
5-10.e: Ongoing Monitoring is embedded in Daily Operations: supervisory reviews, automated alerts, exception reports, daily reconciliations. It provides real-time feedback and catches issues quickly. Separate Evaluations are periodic independent assessments (internal audit reviews, SOX 404 assessments) that provide deeper but less frequent scrutiny.

5-10.q: A Walkthrough in internal control testing involves:
o: Walking through the company's physical premises to check security
Tracing One Transaction from beginning to end through the entire system to understand both design and operating effectiveness
Testing all high-risk transactions in a period
Interviewing all management about control design
5-10.e: Walkthrough: Select One transaction and trace it completely from Initiation to Recording in the financial statements — observing, inspecting documents, and re-performing steps. This tests Both design (would the control work?) and operating effectiveness (is it actually working?). Walkthroughs are required by PCAOB standards for public company audits.

5-10.q: When remediating a control deficiency, the First and most critical step is to:
o: Implement a compensating control immediately
Document the deficiency for the external auditor
Identify the Root Cause — not just the symptom — to design an effective fix
Disclose the deficiency publicly
5-10.e: Effective remediation starts with Root Cause Analysis. If only the symptom is addressed (e.g., one unauthorized transaction), the underlying cause (e.g., no segregation of duties) remains and will generate future failures. Root cause identification leads to a permanent fix. After identifying the root cause, management designs, implements, tests, and monitors the improved control.

5-10.q: Which of the following is an example of Ongoing Monitoring (not a separate evaluation)?
o: Annual internal audit of the accounts payable process
External auditor's assessment of ICFR for SOX 404
A Supervisor Reviewing Exception Reports Of Unusual Transactions Daily As Part Of Their Routine
A self-assessment questionnaire completed by department heads
5-10.e: Ongoing Monitoring is Built Into routine operations — it happens continuously as part of normal business. Daily supervisor review of exception reports = ongoing monitoring. Annual internal audit = separate evaluation. External auditor ICFR assessment = separate evaluation. Self-assessment questionnaire = separate evaluation. Ongoing monitoring is more efficient (continuous feedback) but less rigorous than separate evaluations.

5-10.q: A Separate Evaluation of internal controls differs from ongoing monitoring because it:
o: Is performed every day as part of normal operations
Is always less thorough than ongoing monitoring
Is a Periodic, Independent Assessment — such as an internal audit or SOX 404 assessment — that provides a deeper review than continuous operational monitoring
Is only required when a material weakness is found
5-10.e: Separate Evaluations are periodic independent reviews: (1) more thorough and structured than ongoing monitoring; (2) performed by parties Independent of the function being evaluated (internal audit, external auditor, management assessment teams); (3) provide Deeper insight into design and operating effectiveness. Both types of monitoring are needed — ongoing for continuous feedback, separate for periodic independent depth.

5-10.q: When evaluating internal controls, the Design Effectiveness test asks:
o: Is the control actually being performed consistently by the right people?
Does the person performing the control have enough time to do it?
If The Control Operates As Designed, would it prevent or detect a material misstatement?
How many times has this control failed in the past year?
5-10.e: Test Of Design: asks 'Would this control Work if properly applied?' A poorly designed control cannot prevent/detect errors even if performed perfectly. Example: reconciling the wrong two documents won't catch the risk you're trying to control. Testing design comes First — if design is flawed, there's no point testing operating effectiveness. Walkthroughs are the primary procedure for testing design.

5-10.q: Operating Effectiveness testing of controls evaluates:
o: Whether management has approved the control design
Whether The Control IS Actually Functioning As Designed — performed consistently, by authorized personnel, over the entire period
Only the most recent quarter's control performance
The theoretical ability of the control to prevent misstatements
5-10.e: Test Of Operating Effectiveness: does the control Actually Work in practice? Is it performed: (1) Consistently (not occasionally); (2) by the Right Person (authorized, competent, independent); (3) Throughout The Period (not just at year-end); (4) with the Proper Documentation? Procedures: observation, re-performance, inquiry, inspection of evidence. Even a well-designed control can fail in operation.

5-10.q: The COSO Monitoring component requires that deficiencies identified through monitoring be:
o: Disclosed publicly in all cases
Ignored if they involve only minor processes
Communicated to the appropriate level of management — more significant deficiencies reported to higher levels, including the board
Only fixed if they are material weaknesses
5-10.e: COSO Monitoring: identified deficiencies must be Communicated to the appropriate level. Minor deficiencies = reported to direct management. Significant deficiencies = reported to senior management and audit committee. Material weaknesses = reported to the board/audit committee And disclosed publicly in annual reports (SOX 404). The severity determines the reporting level and urgency of remediation.

5-10.q: A KPI metric showing '95% of reconciliations completed within 3 days of month-end' is useful for monitoring because it:
o: Proves all reconciliations are accurate
Shows that all employees are working efficiently
Measures Whether A Key Detective Control (reconciliations) is being Performed Timely And Consistently — a surrogate measure of control effectiveness
Replaces the need to review reconciliation results
5-10.e: KPIs for Control Monitoring measure whether controls are being executed. Timeliness of reconciliations = a key indicator: late reconciliations are as problematic as no reconciliations (errors accumulate, fraud can be concealed longer). Other control KPIs: % employees completing compliance training, % control exceptions resolved within Sla, # audit findings per period, days to remediate deficiencies.

5-10.q: Root Cause Analysis in control deficiency remediation means:
o: Testing the same controls more frequently
Implementing compensating controls regardless of the underlying issue
Identifying The Fundamental Reason the control failed — not just the visible symptom — to design a permanent fix
Disclosing the deficiency to the public immediately
5-10.e: Root Cause Analysis: if a reconciliation error occurs because one person performs both sides (symptom: error found in reconciliation), the root cause is Lack Of Segregation Of Duties — not a need for more reconciliations. Addressing the root cause (adding a second person or implementing a compensating review) produces a Permanent fix. Addressing only the symptom means the deficiency will recur.

5-10.q: A Self-Assessment questionnaire completed by department managers about their controls is an example of:
o: Ongoing monitoring built into daily operations
An external audit procedure
A Separate Evaluation — a periodic independent-type assessment where management formally reviews their own controls
A test of operating effectiveness by internal audit
5-10.e: Control Self-Assessments (Csa) are Separate Evaluations where management or employees periodically and formally evaluate the controls in their area. They provide management's perspective on control effectiveness, identify gaps, and build control ownership. While not as rigorous as internal audit (the same people who own controls are evaluating them), CSAs are cost-effective and increase control awareness. Internal audit may use Csa results as an input to their risk assessment.

5-10.q: The Severity hierarchy of control deficiencies from Least to Most severe is:
o: Significant Deficiency → Material Weakness → Control Deficiency
Material Weakness → Significant Deficiency → Control Deficiency
Control Deficiency → Significant Deficiency → Material Weakness
No established hierarchy — all deficiencies are equal
5-10.e: Severity hierarchy (least to most severe): (1) Control Deficiency — design or operation doesn't allow detection/prevention (report to management); (2) Significant Deficiency — more serious, warrants attention from governance bodies (report to audit committee and external auditor in writing); (3) Material Weakness — reasonable possibility of material misstatement (must be reported publicly — adverse ICFR opinion results).

5-10.q: Automated Monitoring Controls (system-generated alerts and exception reports) offer which advantage over manual monitoring?
o: They are cheaper to implement in all cases
They require more human judgment
They operate Continuously And Consistently without human error or fatigue — providing real-time detection of threshold breaches
They can replace all manual detective controls
5-10.e: Automated Monitoring advantages: (1) Continuous — runs 24/7 without breaks; (2) Consistent — applies the same rules every time (no human variation); (3) Real-Time — immediate alerts rather than periodic manual review; (4) No Fatigue — doesn't miss items due to distraction. Limitations: only as good as the rules programmed (can miss novel fraud schemes); requires IT controls; alerts can be ignored or suppressed by insiders.

5-10.q: The SOX Section 404 Annual Assessment of ICFR must be performed as of:
o: Each quarter-end
The beginning of the fiscal year
The End Of The Fiscal Year — management evaluates whether ICFR is effective As Of year-end
Monthly throughout the year
5-10.e: SOX 404 Assessment: management evaluates the effectiveness of ICFR As Of the last day of the Fiscal Year (year-end). The assessment is included in the Annual Report (10-K). Quarterly reports (10-Q) under Section 302 require disclosure of any significant control changes, but the full 404 assessment is annual. The year-end date is critical — controls must be working As Of that specific date.

5-10.q: When internal audit identifies a control finding during an audit, the Appropriate follow-up procedure is to:
o: Close the finding immediately after issuance of the audit report
Wait for the next scheduled audit to check on remediation
Track Management'S Remediation Progress and verify that corrective actions were actually implemented by the agreed deadline
Report the finding to the SEC if not fixed within 30 days
5-10.e: Iia Standards require internal audit to Follow Up on findings: (1) track management's Remediation Commitments; (2) verify that corrective actions were implemented By The Agreed Date; (3) assess whether the corrective action actually addresses the root cause; (4) report to the audit committee on remediation status and overdue items. Without follow-up, audit adds no lasting value.

5-10.q: A company discovers that a control has Degraded over time due to staff turnover — the new employee performing the control was never trained on the procedure. This is Best addressed through:
o: Replacing the control with an automated system
Disclosing the gap as a material weakness immediately
Addressing Root Cause: implementing a formal Onboarding and Training process that ensures new employees performing controls understand the procedures before starting
Increasing the frequency of the control
5-10.e: Control Degradation due to personnel changes is common and preventable. Root cause = inadequate onboarding/training for control owners. Fix: formal documented training requirements for all positions with control responsibilities, including sign-off that training was completed before taking over the role. Controls must be re-evaluated whenever key personnel change.

5-10.q: In the COSO framework, which component is Designed To Close The Loop by ensuring the other four components continue to work over time?
o: Control Activities
Risk Assessment
Information & Communication
Monitoring Activities — the 5th component that evaluates whether the other components remain present and functioning
5-10.e: Monitoring Activities (5th COSO component) is the feedback mechanism that ensures the control system remains effective over time. Controls don't maintain themselves — staff change, processes evolve, new risks emerge. Without monitoring, even well-designed controls deteriorate. Monitoring 'closes the loop': it identifies control failures and drives corrective action, ensuring the COSO system is self-correcting.

5-10.q: A company remediates a significant deficiency by implementing a new review process. The Next step after implementation should be:
o: Immediately declare the deficiency remediated in the annual report
Wait until the next annual audit to test the new control
Test The New Control to verify it is designed effectively And operating effectively — then monitor for a sufficient period before concluding it is remediated
Replace the entire control system
5-10.e: After implementing a corrective action: (1) Test Design — does the new control address the root cause? (2) Test Operating Effectiveness — is it actually being performed as designed, consistently, by the right people? (3) Monitor for a Sufficient period (typically at least one or more quarters) before concluding the deficiency is remediated. The external auditor must also concur that the remediation is sufficient.

5-11.q: Which one of the following would most compromise the use of backups as protection against loss or damage of master files?
o: Storing of all files in one location.
Inadequate ventilation.
Use of magnetic tape.
Failure to encrypt data.
5-11.e: Storing all files in one location undermines the concept of multiple backups inherent in the grandparent-parent-child principle.
wrongWhy: Inadequate ventilation is not the greatest risk to using backups as protection against loss or damage of master files.
The use of magnetic tape is not the greatest risk to using backups as protection against loss or damage of master files.
The failure to encrypt data is not the greatest risk to using backups as protection against loss or damage of master files.

5-11.q: An owner of a company has entered into a contract with a hardware vendor ensuring that in the event of a disaster, the vendor would provide the company with computer equipment. The owner has also entered into a lease contract with a building owner ensuring that in the event of a disaster, the company will be provided with an empty floor in the building to allow for the continuation of its business. Which one of the following best describes this disaster recovery plan?
o: Recovery operations center.
Mirrored data center.
Hot site.
Cold site.
5-11.e: This is the description of a cold site. A cold site is a facility where space, electric power, and heating and air conditioning are available and processing equipment can be installed, though the equipment and the necessary telecommunications are not immediately available.
wrongWhy: This situation does not describe a recovery operations center. A recovery operations center is another name for a hot site, or mirrored data center. It is a backup facility that has a computer system similar to the one used regularly. It is fully operational and immediately available, with all necessary telecommunications hookups for online processing. It also has current, live data being replicated to it in real time from the live site by automated data communications.

This situation does not describe a mirrored data center. A mirrored data center, also called a hot site, is a backup facility that has a computer system similar to the one used regularly. The mirrored data center is fully operational and immediately available, with all necessary telecommunications hookups for online processing. It also has current, live data being replicated to it in real time from the live site by automated data communications.

This does not describe a hot site. A hot site, also called a mirrored data center, is a backup facility that has a computer system similar to the one used regularly. The hot site is fully operational and immediately available, with all necessary telecommunications hookups for online processing. It also has current, live data being replicated to it in real time from the live site by automated data communications.

5-11.q: A large property insurance company has regional centers that customers call to report claims. Although the regional centers are not located in areas known to be prone to natural disasters, the company needs a disaster recovery plan that would restore call answering capacity in the event of a disaster or other extended loss of service. The best plan for restoring capacity in the event of a disaster would be to reroute call traffic to
o: A cold site that duplicates regional facilities.
Non-affected regional centers.
A third-party service center.
A hot site that duplicates regional facilities.
5-11.e: Rerouting call traffic to non-affected regional centers is the best approach because it minimizes cost, maximizes the company's control over the reconfiguration, and permits calls to be answered by the company's skilled personnel.
wrongWhy: A cold site is a facility where space, electric power, and heating and air conditioning are available and processing equipment can be installed, though the equipment and the necessary telecommunications are not immediately available. Duplicating regional facilities in a cold site would not immediately provide equipment, software, or personnel.
Rerouting call traffic to a third-party service center would be overly expensive because of personnel cost, and service center personnel would not be trained for the company's calls.

A hot site is a backup facility that has a computer system similar to the one used regularly. The hot site must be fully operational and immediately available, with all necessary telecommunications hookups for online processing. A hot site also has current, live data that is replicated to it from the live site, either by data communications or by on-site storage of backup media. Duplicating regional facilities in a hot site would provide space, equipment, and some software but would not provide personnel.

5-11.q: Testing business continuity plans gives a company confidence that
o: the contingency plans are sufficient to cope with an economic downturn.
business operations should resume quickly in the event of a disaster.
additional financing should be available to remain viable as a going concern.
the company could restore its IT function if the data center is destroyed.
5-11.e: Business continuity planning involves defining the risks facing a company in the event of a disaster, assessing those risks, creating procedures to mitigate those risks, regularly testing those procedures to ensure that they work as expected, and periodically reviewing the procedures to make sure that they are up to date. Business continuity plans should be tested periodically to ensure that they continue to work as expected. To reveal any weaknesses in the plan, the testing should simulate a disaster, and the time to recovery should be recorded. Such testing can give a company confidence that business operations should be able to be resumed quickly in the event of a disaster.
wrongWhy: Business continuity planning is not concerned with coping with an economic downturn. Busness continuity planning involves defining the risks facing a company in the event of a disaster, assessing those risks, creating procedures to mitigate those risks, regularly testing those procedures to ensure that they work as expected, and periodically reviewing the procedures to make sure that they are up to date. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Testing business continuity plans does not give a company confidence that additional financing should be available to remain viable as a going concern. Busness continuity planning involves defining the risks facing a company in the event of a disaster, assessing those risks, creating procedures to mitigate those risks, regularly testing those procedures to ensure that they work as expected, and periodically reviewing the procedures to make sure that they are up to date. To reveal any weaknesses in the plan, the testing should simulate a disaster. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Businesss continuity planning involves more than the restoration of the IT function if the data center is destroyed. Busness continuity planning involves defining the risks facing a company in the event of a disaster, assessing those risks, creating procedures to mitigate those risks, regularly testing those procedures to ensure that they work as expected, and periodically reviewing the procedures to make sure that they are up to date. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-11.q: A company is updating its backup control plan. Which one of the following should be included in this plan?
o: An uninterruptible power system to avoid loss of data from power interruption.
A communication and service restoration plan.
Concurrency controls in multi-user systems.
Limit access to data processing centers to authorized personnel.
5-11.e: A communication restoration and service restoration plan is part of a backup control plan, which is an important part of a disaster recovery plan. It is essential that the company have plans for the backup and recovery of its data, including an alternative site for service restoration and data communications for tranmission of data and online processing in the event of a disaster.
wrongWhy: An uninterruptible power system is an important part of a disaster recovery plan, as is a backup control plan. However, an uninterruptible power system is not a part of the backup control plan. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Concurrency controls are not part of a backup control system. When multiple users try to access the same file or program at the same time, they can destroy each other's work. Concurrency controls in a software program allow the program to deal with multiple users trying to access the same file or program at the same time so work is not destroyed. With concurrency controls in a software application, multiple users can access the same file at the same time without having to worry about data loss; or concurrency controls can also be used to prevent file compromise by restricting access to one user at a time. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Limiting access to data processing centers to authorized personnel is a physical access control that is used to secure equipment and premises. It is not a part of a backup control plan. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-11.q: Contingency plans for information systems should include appropriate backup agreements. Which of the following arrangements would be considered too vendordependent when vital operations require almost immediate availability of computer resources?
o: A "cold and hot site" combination arrangement.
Using excess capacity at another data center within the organization.
A "cold site" arrangement.
A "hot site" arrangement.
5-11.e: A "cold site" is a facility where power and space are available to install processing equipment, but it is not immediately available. If an organization uses a cold site, its disaster recovery plan must include arrangements to get computer equipment installed there quickly. Thus, a "cold site" arrangement is too vendor-dependent because the company has to rely on vendors for timely delivery of the equipment.
wrongWhy: A "cold and hot site" combination arrangement is where the hot site is first used until the cold site is prepared. Thus, it is not too vendor-dependent.
Using excess capacity at another data center within the organization ensures that assets needed for backup are available. Thus, it is not too vendor-dependent.
A "hot site" arrangement must be fully operational and immediately available. A "hot site" arrangement cannot be too vendor-dependent.

5-11.q: A company has in its disaster recovery plan a process to store in a secure, offsite warehouse building a back-up of its current financial operating systems. What type of back-up location is the company most likely using?
o: A hot site.
A recovery operations center.
A cold site.
A mirrored data center.
5-11.e: This is most likely to be a cold site. A cold site is a facility where space, electric power, and heating and air conditioning are available and processing equipment can be installed, though the equipment and the necessary telecommunications are not immediately available. If an organization uses a cold site, its disaster recovery plan must include arrangements to quickly get computer equipment installed and operational.
wrongWhy: This is not the description of a hot site. A hot site is a backup facility that has a computer system similar to the one used regularly. The hot site must be fully operational and immediately available, with all necessary telecommunications hookups for online processing. A hot site also has current, live data that is replicated to it in real-time from the live site by automated data communications.

This is not a description of a recovery operations center. "Recovery operations center" is another term for a hot site. A hot site is a backup facility that has a computer system similar to the one used regularly. The hot site must be fully operational and immediately available, with all necessary telecommunications hookups for online processing. A hot site also has current, live data that is replicated to it in real-time from the live site by automated data communications.

This is not the description of a mirrored data center. A mirrored data center is another term for a hot site. It is a fully operational secondary data center where data is backed up in real-time by automated data communications. It has a computer system similar to the one used regularly. The hot site must be fully operational and immediately available, with all necessary telecommunications hookups for online processing.

5-11.q: When attempting to restore computing facilities at an alternate site following a disaster, which one of the following should be restored first?
o: Batch system.
Decision support system.
Online system.
Operating system.
5-11.e: The operating system should be restored first, because without the operating system, none of the other applications can be run. The operating system serves the following functions: It provides the user interface that allows the user to communicate with the computer in order to load programs, access files, and accomplish other tasks. It provides resource management to manage the hardware and networking resources of the system. It contains file management programs that control the creation, deletion and access of files and also keep track of the physical locations of files on secondary storage devices. It includes task management programs to manage the accomplishment of the computing tasks. This enables multitasking capability, so that several computing tasks, for example keyboarding and printing, can take place at the same time. It provides utilities and support service to perform housekeeping tasks and file conversion functions such as data backup, data recovery, virus protection, data compression and file defragmentation.
wrongWhy: A batch system is not a system to be restored at all. The term "batch system" describes a type of transaction processing system. In a batch system, transactions of a similar type (i.e., accounts receivable receipts) are accumulated over a period of time such as a day, and then the whole batch is processed to update the master files.
A decision support system is not the system to restore first. A decision support system would not be usable if it were restored first.
An online system is not the system to restore first. An online system would not be usable if it were restored first.

5-11.q: Many organizations are critically dependent on information systems to support daily business operations. Consequently, an organization may incur significant loss of revenues or incur significant expenses if a disaster such as a hurricane or power outage causes information systems processing to be delayed or interrupted. A bank, for example, may incur significant penalties as a result of missed payments. Which of the following management activities is essential to ensure continuity of operations in the event a disaster or catastrophe impairs information systems processing?
o: Electronic vaulting.
Change control procedures.
Review of insurance coverage.
Contingency planning.
5-11.e: Contingency planning is a management activity which is essential to ensure continuity of operations in the event a disaster impairs information systems processing.
wrongWhy: Electronic vaulting is a technology that may be used to address contingency planning issues.
Change control procedures do not ensure continuity of operations.
Review of insurance coverage is an aspect of risk analysis, and a much narrower concept than contingency planning.

5-11.q: The primary objective of a disaster recovery plan is to
o: set up and be able to operate a cold site if disaster damages important assets.
plan for a disaster that may destroy the building with an alternative office space.
minimize the effects of a disaster on business operations and productivity.
ensure employees and assets are safe when a disaster affects the business.
5-11.e: This is the primary objective of a disaster recovery plan. Without the ability to recover from a disaster, a company could be out of business.
wrongWhy: This is not the primary objective of a disaster recovery plan. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
This is not the primary objective of a disaster recovery plan. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
This is not the primary objective of a disaster recovery plan. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-11.q: A critical aspect of a disaster recovery plan is to be able to regain operational capability as soon as possible. In order to accomplish this, an organization can have an arrangement with its computer hardware vendor to have a fully operational facility available that is configured to the user's specific needs. This is best known as a(n)
o: Cold site.
Parallel system.
Uninterruptible power system.
Hot site.
5-11.e: A hot site is a backup facility that has a computer system that is similar to the one used regularly and is fully operational and thus immediately available.
wrongWhy: A cold site is a facility that can be used to install computer equipment if needed, but it is not fully operational.
A parallel system is a system that is identical to the main system.
An uninterruptible power system (UPS) is a backup power source that kicks in automatically in the event of a power outage to prevent loss of data.

5-11.q: A company is following a plan to continue operations if a place of business is affected by disasters. The plan is most likely called
o: strategic planning.
business continuity planning.
crisis communication planning.
continuous availability planning.
5-11.e: This is a definition of a business continuity plan. Business continuity planning involves defining the risks facing a company in the event of a disaster, assessing those risks, creating procedures to mitigate those risks, regularly testing those procedures to ensure that they work as expected, and periodically reviewing the procedures to make sure that they are up to date.
wrongWhy: This is not a description of strategic planning. Strategic planning is the formulation of strategies, or actions, to take to increase the company's performance.

This is not a crisis communication plan. A crisis communication plan is the policies and procedures for the coordination of communications both internally and externally in the event of an emergency or a negative situation that requires a public response, such as the accessing of confidential company data by a hacker.
