This is not a description of continuous availability planning. Continuous availability describes the information technology methods used to ensure business continuity in the event of a disaster. While continuous availability is an important part of a plan to continue operations if a place of business is affected by a disaster, it is not the full plan.

5-11.q: A company’s management is aware that it cannot foresee every contingency even with the best planning. Management believes, however, that a more thorough recovery plan increases the ability to resume operations quickly after an interruption and thus to:
o: Receive the maximum benefit from planning.
Maintain the same level of employment.
Fulfill its obligations to customers.
Minimize the cost of facility repair.
5-11.e: The better the recovery plans, the more likely the company would be to resume operations quickly and fulfill its obligations to customers.
wrongWhy: The maximum benefit from planning is that it prompts action to avoid the most likely or most devastating events with the potential to interrupt business. Management would be delighted if planning ensured that business was never interrupted and thus that the recovery plan was never invoked.
The company may or may not maintain the same level of employment after a disaster. For example, a disaster that destroys productive capacity in one plant may lead to layoffs.
Thorough planning may or may not minimize the cost of facility repair. That is, the best approach may be to undergo more expensive repair sooner in order to resume operations sooner.

5-11.q: An auditing firm is concluding its audit of a publicly-traded company in the U.S. After obtaining sufficient audit evidence, an external auditor concludes that misstatements in the aggregate are material but not pervasive to the financial statements. What type of opinion should the external auditor issue on the company’s financial statements?
o: Adverse opinion.
Unmodified opinion.
Disclaimer of opinion.
Qualified opinion.
5-11.e: This is a description of a situation in which a qualified opinion should be issued. A qualified opinion contains an exception, meaning that the financial statements do not present a true and fair picture. However, the exception is usually not significant enough to cause the statements as a whole to be misleading to the point that they should not be used, although it does prevent the auditor from issuing an unqualified opinion. Usually, a qualified opinion is issued under one of these conditions: 1. the scope of the auditor’s examination (that is, the work that the auditor wanted to perform) was limited or was affected by restrictions, or 2. The statements do not present fairly the company’s financial position or results of operations because of a lack of conformity with generally accepted accounting principles or because of inadequate disclosures.
wrongWhy: An adverse opinion is issued when the exceptions are so material that, in the auditor’s judgment, a qualified opinion is not appropriate. This means that the financial statements, taken as a whole, are not presented in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles. The situation described here is not one where an adverse audit opinion would be appropriate.

An unmodified audit opinion is another term for an unqualified audit opinion. An unmodified/unqualified audit report is a “clean” audit report. The auditor expresses the opinion that the financial statements present fairly, in all material respects, the financial position, results of operations, and cash flows of the company, in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles. The situation described here is not one where an unqualified/unmodified audit opinion would be appropriate.

A disclaimer of opinion is used when the auditor has not been able to gather enough information on the financial statements to express an opinion or if the auditor is not independent of the client. The situation described here is not one where a disclaimer of audit opinion would be appropriate.

5-11.q: A U.S. publicly-traded company that is classified by the SEC as an accelerated filer completed its annual audit and internal control assessment. Its external auditor attested to the financial statements by giving an audit opinion but did not include an opinion on the effectiveness of the company's internal control over financial reporting. Did the auditor violate PCAOB requirements for audit opinions?
o: Yes, because the company did not include a certification by the CFO and CEO.
No, but the company has violated Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act.
Yes, because the external auditor did not attest to and report on the effectiveness of the company's internal control over financial reporting.
No, the auditor was still in compliance due to the safe harbor rules.
5-11.e: The external auditor has violated PCAOB requirements. PCAOB Audit Standard 2201 requires audits of publicly-traded companies categorized by the SEC as "accelerated" or "large accelerated" filers to include an opinion by the external auditor regarding the effectiveness of the company's internal control over financial reporting (ICFR). The auditor's internal control opinion is to be integrated with the auditor's opinion on the financial statements.
wrongWhy: The PCAOB does not require a certification by the CFO and CEO. The PCAOB regulates the company's external auditor, not the company. The requirement for the CFO and CEO to provide certifications is contained in Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act requires that each quarterly or annual report submitted to the SEC include certifications by the company's principal executive officer and its principal financial officer indicating that (1) the officer has reviewed the report, (2) the report does not contain any untrue material misstatement or omission that would cause the report to be misleading, and (3) to the best of the officer's knowledge, the financial statements and all other related information in the report fairly present in all material respects the financial condition and results of operations of the company for all the periods presented in the report.

The external auditor has violated PCAOB requirements, but we are given no information to determine whether the company was or was not in violation of Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. Section 302 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act requires that each quarterly or annual report submitted to the SEC include certifications by the company's principal executive officer and its principal financial officer indicating that (1) the officer has reviewed the report, (2) the report does not contain any untrue material misstatement or omission that would cause the report to be misleading, and (3) to the best of the officer's knowledge, the financial statements and all other related information in the report fairly present in all material respects the financial condition and results of operations of the company for all the periods presented in the report.

SEC rules for public company reporting contain safe-harbor provisions that protect management from liability for making financial projections and forecasts made in good faith. Safe-harbor rules do not protect an auditor from violation of a PCAOB requirement.

5-11.q: An external auditor discovers that a payroll supervisor of the firm being audited has misappropriated $10,000. The firm's total assets and before-tax net income are $14 million and $3 million, respectively. Assuming there are no other issues that will affect the report, the external auditor's report would most likely contain a(n)
o: Disclaimer of opinion.
Scope qualification.
Unqualified opinion.
Adverse opinion.
5-11.e: The question does not state that the statements do not disclose the loss, so we can assume that the financial statements report the loss correctly. Furthermore, the misappropriation is an immaterial amount to the company as a whole. Therefore, the financial statements would present fairly, in all material respects, the financial position, results of operations, and cash flows of the company in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles, and the auditor would be able to issue an unqualified opinion.
wrongWhy: A disclaimer of opinion is given when the auditor is not independent or there has been a large scope limitation and the auditor has not been able to gather enough information on the financial statements to express an opinion. As neither of these conditions exists, a disclaimer of opinion would not be appropriate.

A scope limitation occurs when a test that the auditor would like to perform is not able to be performed. This situation does not exist so there would be to reason to issue an opinion that mentions a scope limitation.

An adverse opinion is issued when the exceptions are so material that, in the auditor's judgment, the financial statements do not present fairly the financial position of the company in accordance with GAAP. This is an immaterial item and since it does not even state that this was not disclosed, an adverse opinion would not be appropriate.

5-11.q: When assessing a company’s internal control structure policies and procedures, the primary consideration is whether they
o: reflect management's philosophy and operating style.
prevent management override.
affect the financial statement assertions.
relate to the control environment.
5-11.e: Financial statement assertions are claims made by management in presenting information in the financial statements, such as net income for the period or total liabilities as of the financial statement date. Section 404 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act requires each annual report filed with the SEC to contain an assessment by management of the adequacy of the company’s internal control over financial reporting. The internal control report must (1) state the responsibility of management for establishing and maintaining an adequate internal control structure and procedures for financial reporting; and (2) contain an assessment of the effectiveness of the internal control structure and procedures of the company for financial reporting as of the end of its most recent fiscal year. The primary consideration of management in this assessment is how well the company’s internal control policies and procedures provide adequate internal control over financial reporting, in order to enable fulfillment of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act requirements.
wrongWhy: Management's philosophy and operating style is one component of the control environment, but it is not the primary consideration of management in its assessment of the adequacy of the company's internal control. The primary consideration of management in assessing the company's internal control is how well the company’s internal control policies and procedures provide adequate internal control over financial reporting. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The primary consideration in performing an assessment of a company's internal control is not to prevent management override. Management override cannot be prevented. Since the company's management is responsible for designing, implementing, and maintaining its internal control system, management has the opportunity to override the controls. To override a control, a manager would simply need to instruct a subordinate to not perform that control activity. The potential for management override of the controls is a major risk factor that the audit committee of the board of directors must take into consideration as part of their responsibility for the oversight of management. The primary consideration of management in assessing the company's internal control is how well the company's internal controls provide control over financial reporting. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The control environment is very important to internal control, but it is not the primary consideration in assessing the company’s internal control structure policies and procedures. The primary consideration of management in assessing the company's internal control is how well the company's internal controls provide control over financial reporting. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

5-11.q: One of the financial statement auditor's major concerns is to ascertain whether internal control is designed to provide reasonable assurance that
o: Corporate morale problems are addressed immediately and effectively.
The chief accounting officer reviews all accounting transactions.
Profit margins are maximized, and operational efficiency is optimized.
Financial reporting is reliable.
5-11.e: Internal control is a method, or process, that is carried out by an entity's board of directors, management, and other personnel, and designed to provide reasonable assurance that objectives in the following four categories will be achieved: (1) effectiveness and efficiency of operations; (2) reliability of financial reporting; (3) compliance with applicable laws and regulations; and (4) safeguarding of assets. The concerns of the financial statement auditor will relate to no. 2, reliability of financial reporting.
wrongWhy: Corporate morale problems are not relevant to a financial statement audit.
It is not necessary that the chief accounting officer review all accounting transactions. Therefore, this is not one of a financial statement auditor's major concerns.
While it is important to maximize profits and optimize operational efficiency, this is not one of a financial statement auditor's major concerns.

5-11.q: An audit of the financial statements of Camden Corporation is being conducted by an external auditor. The external auditor is expected to
o: Critique the wisdom and legality of Camden's business decisions.
Express an opinion as to the attractiveness of Camden for investment purposes.
Certify to the correctness of Camden's financial statements.
Express an opinion as to the fairness of Camden's financial statements.
5-11.e: The external auditor has a responsibility to conduct an independent examination of the accounting data prepared and presented by management and express an opinion on the financial statements as to whether or not they have been prepared in accordance with GAAP.
wrongWhy: The external auditor has a responsibility to conduct an independent examination of the accounting data prepared and presented by management and express an opinion on the financial statements as to whether or not they have been prepared in accordance with GAAP. The auditor does not address the wisdom or legality of the company's decisions.

The external auditor has a responsibility to conduct an independent examination of the accounting data prepared and presented by management and express an opinion on the financial statements as to whether or not they have been prepared in accordance with GAAP. The auditor does not express an opinion about the attractiveness of the company as an investment.

The external auditor has a responsibility to conduct an independent examination of the accounting data prepared and presented by management and express an opinion on the financial statements as to whether or not they have been prepared in accordance with GAAP, not certify to the correctness of the client's financial statements. Though this is a minor linguistic difference, the auditor does not certify whether the financial statements are correct, but rather states whether or not they are free from material errors. The word correct is not a word that auditors use.

5-11.q: The Recovery Time Objective (Rto) defines:
o: How much data can be lost in a disaster
The maximum acceptable time to Restore a system after failure
The cost of a disaster recovery site
The minimum backup frequency required
5-11.e: Rto = the maximum Acceptable Time to restore a system or process after a failure. If Rto is 4 hours, the system must be fully operational within 4 hours of a disruption. It drives decisions about what type of recovery site and backup approach to use.
